I saw a Facebook post accusing Leandro Leviste of corruption. 

Facebook posts like that are easy to make, especially from a faceless page. 

I’m not satisfied with shallow memes and posts with zero context, so let’s do a deep dive and find out: is Leandro Leviste corrupt?

When I think about that question, I realize I’m not just reacting to him, I’m reacting to a pattern I’ve seen a countless  of times: some well-connected guy branded as “reformer,” and suddenly my feed is a mix of infographics, outrage threads, and “Receipts!!!” posts. 

Some of them are legit. Some of them are clearly hit jobs. So instead of choosing a side instantly, I find myself asking: what exactly did this guy do, and what’s just noise people are piling on?

🟥 The Cabral files: hero move or something darker?

The part that really complicates Leviste for me is the “Cabral files” scandal. 

On the surface, it sounds simple: a young congressman says he has digital files from the late DPWH Undersecretary Catalina Cabral that supposedly expose big corruption in flood control projects. 

It’s the perfect reformist story—young, rich guy turns on the system and exposes the decay.

But then the cracks start showing. 

The Ombudsman publicly said Leviste didn’t give them the complete set of files, only “limited portions,” which already makes me wonder: if you’re crusading for transparency, why are you selectively sharing evidence? 

Malacañang also wants to know how he even got those files in the first place, and there are now calls to investigate whether his acquisition of the documents was legal and whether the files themselves are authentic and complete.

So now I’m stuck between two conflicting images in my head. 

On one side: Leviste as whistleblower, taking on a DPWH system where an engineer was literally arrested in an entrapment operation after allegedly trying to offer him a cut of project kickbacks. 

On the other: Leviste as power player, holding potentially sensitive government data, releasing only the parts that suit his story, and being less transparent with investigators than he is with the cameras. 

That’s where the question of corruption for me becomes less about “Is he brave?” and more about “Is he playing with state information like a weapon?”

🟥 The billionaire who “built nothing”

Then there’s the business side, which is its own rabbit hole. 

The more I read, the more I bump into this phrase people are throwing around online: “the billionaire who built nothing.” 

The gist is this: 

Leviste’s company SPNEC went public at ₱1 per share, promising massive solar projects and becoming the first “pure solar play” on the local exchange. 

Over the years, he secured huge deals—including a giant project called Terra Solar—and then gradually sold his shares to Meralco’s renewable arm, MGreen, for around ₱13.76 billion under successive deals.

On paper, that’s a baller entrepreneur story: start a company, raise money, take risks, then exit to a bigger player. 

But critics point out a different angle: that while he earned tens of billions of pesos on paper from selling SPNEC and related assets to Meralco’s group, only a fraction of the promised capacity has actually been built and delivering power so far. 

It starts to look less like “I built this empire” and more like “I assembled permits, franchises, and future promises, then sold the bundle to an oligarch.”

So I ask myself: is that corruption, or just capitalism on steroids? 

It’s legal to sell a company based on future potential. 

It’s also very convenient when you have the right last name, access to lawmakers, and friends in agencies that approve energy deals. 

The law may not call that graft, but from the outside, it still feels like a game regular Filipinos are never allowed to play.

🟥 Nepo baby politics and the smell of conflict of interest

I can’t think about Leviste without thinking about his last name. 

He’s not some random kid from nowhere. He’s Loren Legarda’s son, and she’s been one of the key names associated with climate, environment, and policy work for years. 

It’s not illegal to be the child of a powerful politician, but when that child ends up holding huge energy franchises and negotiating with giants like Meralco, the story of “independent businessman” starts to feel thin.

Add to that the older controversies around franchises and monopolies in the renewable space, and the pattern feels familiar: laws and franchises that are technically legal, passed through proper channels, but structured in a way that gives one politically connected player a huge edge.

You look at that and think: even if every document is in order, is this what public service is supposed to look like? 

A revolving door where mom helps shape the playground, and the son owns half the swings?

🟥 So where do I land on “is he corrupt”?

After thinking about all this, my answer isn’t as clean as a meme, and maybe that’s the point. 

If by “corrupt” I mean “already convicted of plunder or graft,” that’s not where Leandro Leviste is right now. There’s no court decision saying he looted public coffers.

But if by “corrupt” I mean someone who operates in that gray zone where political capital, insider access, and state power get mixed into private deals and selective transparency, then yes—the case against him feels heavy. 

He profits from a system of oligarchic capitalism, flips assets built on public contracts and regulatory favors, and now stands at the center of a scandal over sensitive government files that he hasn’t fully turned over to investigators while projecting himself as the face of anti-corruption.

So the next time I see a Facebook post screaming “Leviste is corrupt,” I’m not just going to scroll past it, but I’m also not going to treat it as the final word.

For me, the more honest way to say it is this: Leandro Leviste is not just one guy with allegations; he is a walking example of how power, money, and law blur together in the Philippines. 

And that, in itself, already tells me something is deeply off with this kid, even if the courtroom hasn’t caught up yet.

So, I will keep watching  him.

🟥 The Last Morning of Dr. Jose Rizal: An MCT Reimagining

When I think about the last twenty‑four hours of Dr. Jose Rizal’s life, the records I’ve read—timelines, letters, eyewitness accounts—come to me as scattered fragments. 

This is my reimagining of that final day, a story stitched from those bits and pieces and filled in with my own creative sense of how it might have felt to walk beside him toward the morning of his death.

🟥 Before dawn in Fort Santiago

Inside his cell at Fort Santiago, the night feels used up. 

The air is thick, saturated with the smell of damp stone, stale sweat, and the faint sourness of old iron bars that have seen too many hands and too many endings. 

A small alcohol lamp burns on the table, its flame sharp and steady, giving off that thin, stinging scent of fuel that settles at the back of the throat.

He has not slept.

The scratch of his pen on paper is the loudest sound in the room. 

Each stroke feels deliberate, almost stubborn, as if he refuses to let his last lines be anything less than precise. 

On the table sits that unremarkable lamp, the one no guard thinks twice about. 

Rizal knows it holds more than flame. He has already slipped folded pages inside it—his final poem, the one he has carried in his head for days like a secret heartbeat.

“Tell them to look in the lamp,” he has written in careful English for his beloved sister, knowing the guards will not catch the meaning. 

That lamp, warm from its own working, now guards Mi Último Adiós, his last farewell to a country he will not see free.

The walls sweat alongside him, beads of moisture catching the lamplight. 

Somewhere beyond the cell, boots scrape on stone, someone coughs, iron rattles. Manila outside is mostly asleep, but in here the night refuses to end.

🟥 A mother’s entrance

The corridor smells of limewash and old damp, and under that, something faintly medicinal from the priests and doctors who have come and gone through these halls. 

When Teodora Alonso approaches, the rhythm of footsteps changes—slower, uneven, weighted not just with age but with a mother’s dread.

She steps into the cell and the world narrows.

For a heartbeat she sees both versions of him at once: the little boy with ink on his fingers and the man in prison clothes, his wrists rubbed raw from rope. 

Her throat tightens. 

The urge to collapse into him, to claw at fate, rises like a flood. But she sees his face—composed, luminous in the thin light—and swallows everything that would break him.

He kneels and kisses her hand.

Leather creaks as his shoes bend. His lips are warm on her skin. 

It is a gesture they have shared before, but here it feels like a sacrament. 

No wailing, no bargaining. 

To an onlooker, it might look rehearsed, but inside both of them everything is unraveling in slow motion.

He speaks softly; later accounts will say he asked her to accept his fate, to be at peace, to forgive those who wronged them. 

In his head he is memorizing this last physical contact—mild perfume, rice powder, the slight tremor in her fingers. 

She leaves the cell without looking back because she knows if she turns, she will not be able to walk away.

🟥 Mass, confession, and the strange calm

Around three in the morning, a priest arrives. 

Latin fills the cramped space, familiar syllables layered over the smell of oil, wax, and the fading tang of the lamp. 

Rizal kneels, confesses, receives Holy Communion.

Later, the army doctor checks his pulse.

Perhaps he expects chaos beneath the skin: an erratic drumbeat, a heart throwing itself against the ribs in protest. 

Instead he finds a steady, almost unnervingly regular rhythm.

The doctor waits for the wild flutter he knows so well from other men; under Rizal’s skin, the beat stays stubbornly even, as if his body has already agreed to what the morning demands.

Outside, the gray of dawn is soaking into the sky.

🟥 Dressing for the firing squad

By half past five, he dresses.

He chooses a fine black woolen suit, a white vest, shirt, tie, and a small derby hat. Nothing on him says “criminal.” 

The fabric is clean, pressed; the overall impression is not of a man being marched to death but of a gentleman preparing for a day’s work.

The black wool catches the thin light, the white vest a narrow brightness at his chest. 

He smooths his lapel as far as the rope allows, a movement that would look more at home in a clinic corridor than in a prison yard.

Beside him, uniforms hang loose on tired bodies, creased with sweat and lost sleep, while his suit sits on him as if he were heading to a clinic, not a firing squad.

He picks up the rosary. The beads fit into his palm like a familiar sentence; his thumb finds grooves worn by use. The rope bites lightly into the skin of his wrists as the guards bind his hands behind his back. He exhales, slow.

🟥 The walk through a waking city

When he steps out of Fort Santiago, the air outside is cool but heavy, carrying salt from Manila Bay and smoke from early cooking fires. The sky is a low, indecisive gray. Stone underfoot is slick with dawn moisture.

The escort forms around him—Spanish officers at the front, Filipino volunteers in coarse cañamo uniforms at the sides, and among them a slight, dark-haired twenty-year-old in the drum corps named Hilarion Eustaquio Martinez of the Leales Voluntarios de Manila, his drum hanging unused at his hip as he’s pulled into duty that needs rifles more than rhythm.

They walk.

The rhythm of boots on stone surrounds him, punctuated by the soft clink of metal buckles, the faint rattle of a scabbard knocking against a thigh. 

From side streets, vendors pushing carts pause, recognizing his profile, then lower their eyes. 

A few make the sign of the cross. 

A woman starts forward, fingers lifting off the doorframe, then pulls herself back into the shadow and grips the wood until her knuckles pale.

Rizal’s lips move. 

Martinez will later recall him murmuring, perhaps remembering his school days at the Ateneo, perhaps praying in his own way. 

Single images flicker through his mind: the chalk-dusted classroom, distant European streets, the faces of friends scattered across continents.

Martinez’s own heart is pounding. 

He feels the weight of the rifle that has replaced his drum that morning; the wood is rough where years of fingers have worn it smooth. 

He tells himself this is duty, that he is a soldier, that this is just another order. 

But each time he glances sideways at the man in the black suit, doubt gnaws.

🟥 Bagumbayan at dawn

Bagumbayan opens before them: a broad field edged by trees and the distant glint of the bay. 

The grass is damp, darkened in patches by dew and the many feet that have already trampled it into submission. 

The air smells of wet earth, salt, and the bitter note of gunpowder from cartridges being prepared.

Ten companies of infantry are there. 

Filipinos in the inner ring, rifles ready. Spaniards behind them, weapons aimed not at Rizal but at their colonial subjects, barrels resting on Filipino backs as much as on their rifles.

Spectators cluster behind barricades—officials in pressed uniforms, a few civilians, some women with handkerchiefs pressed to their mouths. 

Photographers stand perilously still beside their boxy cameras, calculating light and angle. The crowd hums in low tones. No one wants to miss anything, and no one wants to truly see it.

Rizal steps into the open.

The ground is uneven; his shoe catches slightly in a dip but he regains balance, shoulders squared. 

The black suit sets him apart from the khaki and blue-gray of military cloth. The rosary in his hand is invisible to most, but very present to him.

Somewhere just behind the line of bayonets, Martinez stands with the rifle he would rather not be carrying, close enough to see the angle of Rizal’s jaw and the way his shoulders stay level.

🟥 The doctor and the denied request

The army surgeon, Dr. Felipe Ruiz Castillo, approaches and gently takes Rizal’s wrist. 

The touch is clinical, but there’s a human curiosity behind it: what does a man’s body do when he knows death is minutes away? 

The pulse tells him: it stays steady, at least in this one man. The doctor steps back with a quiet astonishment he will later struggle to explain.

Rizal asks to face the firing squad. He wants to look his executioners in the eye, to die with his chest toward the bullets rather than his back. 

The request is refused; the position, like the verdict, has already been decided.

He presses for one concession: to turn as he falls. To use his last fraction of strength to twist, so his body will land facing the sky, not the soil. 

This small request is granted. 

The officer’s answer is curt, but not a full refusal; Rizal lowers his head slightly, as if marking out this narrow space where he can still choose how his body will meet the ground.

A Jesuit priest hurries toward him with a crucifix. 

For the clergy, this is the last chance to claim him symbolically. 

He turns his head away. His gaze touches the metal Christ for a heartbeat, then slips off toward the strip of sky above the bay, leaving the crucifix hanging in the air between them.

The crowd quiets. Even the wind seems to hesitate.

🟥 The moment of the rifle volley

The officer raises his saber.

In the inner circle, Filipino soldiers lift their rifles. Some are silently begging that their guns carry blanks. Sweat slides down necks under stiff collars. A few men swallow hard, fingers tightening on the trigger as they feel the Spaniards’ eyes on their backs. 

Behind them, the Spaniards stand ready, rifles angled just a little too comfortably toward the men in front.

Time elongates.

Martinez’s finger is slick with sweat on the trigger. His stomach clenches. He hears the officer’s voice as if from far away.

“Fuego!”

The explosion of sound hits like a wall. Eight rifles discharge. Smoke blooms, hot and acrid, stinging eyes and throats. Birds launch from trees, shrieking into the new light.

Bullets slam into Rizal’s body. 

The impact tears the breath from his lungs; for a split second his chest feels like it has caved in. 

He had been waiting for this exact instant. 

He twists, forcing his body to obey him one last time. Muscles strain, tendons pull; the world tilts. He half-turns as strength slides out of his limbs.

He falls facing the sky.

His derby hat tumbles ahead of him, landing alone on the damp grass. 

The ground is cool and wet against his cheek. The smell of earth, of his own blood, of gunpowder and sweat floods his senses. 

Somewhere in the stunned silence, a dog barks and runs nervously near the body, as if summoned by the commotion.

An officer steps forward and fires the tiro de gracia, the coup de grâce, into his body at close range.

The echo of gunfire lingers in the air. The crowd exhales as if they have been holding their breath for hours.

🟥 The field after everyone leaves

Eventually soldiers move the body. 

The crowd disperses; voices rise again, brittle and loud in the wake of the silence. 

The field remains, scarred by footprints, by compressed grass, by a darker patch of soil where his blood soaked into the ground.

The smell of gunpowder fades, leaving behind dampness, earth, and a faint metallic tang that lingers where he fell.

For most, the spectacle is over. For his family, it is just beginning.

🟥 Handkerchiefs and blood

Later, one of his sisters comes to Bagumbayan with a bundle of fine piña handkerchiefs. They smell faintly of starch and the inside of a chest where such delicate things are kept.

She finds the place.

The earth is still marked, dark and sticky in places where the blood has not yet fully disappeared into the soil. 

She kneels, the hem of her skirt drinking up moisture and dirt. The morning has grown warmer; the air presses down on her shoulders. Her hands tremble at first, then steady with purpose.

One by one, she presses the handkerchiefs onto the ground, letting each piece of cloth soak in that dark stain. 

The piña drinks it greedily, turning from pale translucence to a dense, rust-colored patch that clings damply to her fingers. 

These handkerchiefs were made for parlors and Sunday dresses; now they fold with a sticky resistance, as if they no longer belong to ordinary days. The smell of iron rises from the earth, sharp and intimate.

Around ten to twelve handkerchiefs pass through her hands this way. 

When she is done, she folds each one with care, as if she is wrapping a part of him she refuses to surrender to the state. 

Later, these bloodied squares will be given to family members.

Years later, in one house, a maid will proudly explain how she scrubbed and boiled the stubborn stain away. Somewhere else, another square of cloth remains dark and stiff, tucked away and guarded.

🟥 Bones, brain, and the long keeping

When his remains are eventually exhumed from Paco Cemetery, they are not merely reburied and forgotten. The family and the nation begin the uneasy work of keeping what is left.

Fragments of his brain end up in a small crystal bottle with a silver cap, preserved by alcohol and by the devotion of his eldest sister, Saturnina. 

The bottle is kept with her treasured things—a coral rosary, a hair comb, jewelry—like just another heirloom, except that it once housed the organ that imagined the Philippines free.

Elsewhere, at the Rizal Shrine in Fort Santiago, a single vertebra from his spine rests in a reliquary. 

A chip on one side is said to mark the passage of one of the bullets that killed him. 

Visitors lean close, faces reflected faintly in the glass as they stare at the bone. 

It looks ordinary and yet unbearable: this small, pale curve once stood upright inside the man whose name is now carved into monuments.

Family photos and stories tell of Teodora, years later, lifting his skull from an urn and showing it to visitors when her mood allowed. A mother holding the hollowed head of a son executed by the state.

🟥 Walking beside him

When I think about that last morning now, it doesn’t feel like a neat chapter in a history book. It feels like a room crowded with different minds, each one trying to make sense of the same unfolding tragedy.

In his mind, Rizal is carrying a kind of private ledger—on one side the faces of his family, the memory of quiet meals, the hands that raised him; on the other, the knowledge that he will not walk away from Bagumbayan alive. He knows he did not lead the revolt with a bolo in hand, but he also knows his words have lit fires in people he will never meet. 

And still he walks toward the field, toward the rifles, step after step.

His mother, somewhere else in the city, is left with a jumble of images she cannot put down: the boy with ink-stained fingers, the young man leaving for Europe, the prisoner kissing her hand in a dim cell. 

All those versions of him now fold into a single, unbearable thought—that his body is lying in the open air, cooling under the same sky that once watched him play as a child.

His sister, the one kneeling in the grass with handkerchiefs, is not thinking in grand ideas at all. 

Her world has narrowed to cloth and soil: press, lift, fold; again and again, until the bundle in her hands feels heavier than its size allows. 

She cannot change the verdict or the volley, but she can take this—these stains, this evidence that he was here and bled on this ground—and carry it home.

I keep coming back to the young volunteer, Hilarion Martinez—the drummer who spent that morning holding a rifle instead of sticks. 

I imagine him lying awake years later, replaying the walk from Fort Santiago, the feel of the wood under his palms, the shock of watching Rizal twist to face the sky. 

The sound of “Fuego” must have stayed with him long after the smoke cleared.

And then there is the crowd—officials, bystanders, women with handkerchiefs pressed to their mouths—realizing, slowly and probably against their will, that they are not just watching a sentencing carried out. 

They are standing on a fault line. 

Years later their children will ask, “Where were you when Rizal was shot?” and they will have to remember which side of the rope they stood on.

The rifles lined up against Filipino backs, the refusal to let him face the squad, the careful choreography of uniforms and orders—those choices say more than any proclamation about what that morning was meant to be.

But what lingers for me are smaller details that refuse to bow to that script: the calm beat under the doctor’s fingers; the poem tucked into a lamp; the last twist of his body toward the light; a sister folding away blood-darkened linen; a tiny bottle holding fragments of his brain; a chipped vertebra behind glass; a mother lifting her son’s skull in her hands.

After his death, the poem in the lamp begins its quiet journey from hand to hand. People murmur his final words—“Consummatum est,” it is finished—and retell the way he walked, the way he stood, the way he fell. 

The revolution, already smoldering in the background, draws in his last breath like air and burns hotter.

The field at Bagumbayan dries. Grass grows over the patch where his blood darkened the soil. Manila Bay keeps breathing its salt-heavy air across the city as if nothing has changed.

And yet everything has.

When I follow him in my mind—from the sweating walls of Fort Santiago to that open field by the water—I feel the weight of the morning settle on my own chest. 

The dampness of the air, the sting of powder, the metallic smell of blood and rust, the silence before the order. 

For all the bones and relics and monuments left behind, the thing that stays with me most is not just that he died, but how he chose to stand in those last hours, as if to say, without words: you cannot tell the whole story of me.

The body falls. The story keeps walking.

🟥 December 2025: The Anti‑Dynasty Bill’s “Big Moment” That Wasn’t

[MCT | Opinion]

For a short stretch in December 2025, it really looked like the Philippines might finally be serious about political dynasties. 

The anti‑dynasty bill landed on the president’s priority list, House leaders filed their own version, and the headlines made it sound like a turning point. 

By the end of the month, the picture looked familiar again: big talk, slow movement, and a bill that still leaves dynasties very comfortable.

🟥 Marcos Puts the Anti‑Dynasty Bill on His Priority List

On December 9, 2025, President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. met with congressional leaders in a LEDAC meeting and named four bills he wanted prioritized: an Anti‑Political Dynasty Bill, an Independent People’s Commission Act, a Party‑List System Reform Act, and the CADENA (Citizens’ Access and Disclosure of Expenditures for National Accountability) Act. 

Malacañang framed this as a response to public anger over corruption and political families clinging to power, saying people are asking for a fairer system, especially after the latest scandals. 

For a president who comes from one of the country’s biggest political clans, simply putting “anti‑dynasty bill” on the list was already loaded with symbolism.

The more important part here is what they didn’t do. 

These measures were tagged as “priority,” but none of them—including the anti‑dynasty bill—were certified as “urgent,” which means Congress can still move as slowly as it wants.

🟥 The Dy–Sandro Marcos Version: HB 6771

The day after that meeting, House Speaker Faustino “Bojie” Dy III and House Majority Leader Sandro Marcos filed House Bill No. 6771 and marketed it as an anti‑political dynasty measure. 

On paper, HB 6771 says spouses and relatives up to the fourth civil degree—parents, children, siblings, cousins, in‑laws—cannot hold elective posts at the same time in the same local government unit and at the same level, supposedly to stop one family from crowding a single LGU. 

It went through first reading and was sent to the House committee on suffrage and electoral reforms chaired by Lanao del Sur Rep. Zia Alonto Adiong, who said he would start hearings in January 2026 and roll this together with other anti‑dynasty bills, including the tougher Akbayan‑backed HB 5905.

On the surface, that sequence looks like movement: the president calls it a priority, the House files something, the committee sets itself up as the place where a “real” bill will be put together. 

The details push the story in another direction and the move ends up looking more about optics than about actually cutting into dynasty power.

🟥 Why HB 6771 Doesn’t Read as a Real Anti‑Dynasty Bill

Looking at the criticisms of HB 6771, a clear pattern appears. 

The bill only blocks relatives from sitting at the same time in the same LGU and level, but still allows a clan to spread relatives across different cities, municipalities, provinces, and branches of government and keep control that way. 

It does not touch the usual rotation tactic either—families can take turns every three years so the same surname keeps the position while technically following the rules. 

It also barely touches the way dynasties have moved into party‑list seats and national positions, because its focus is narrow and mostly local.

By mid‑December, many assessments reached the same conclusion: HB 6771 does not break dynasties; it teaches them how to rearrange their chairs. 

Considering that it was filed by a Speaker with multiple relatives in government and a Marcos whose clan dominates both national and local positions, the bill’s softness underlines the conflict of interest.

🟥 The Stronger Alternative Sitting Right Beside It

HB 6771 also has to be seen alongside another bill.  

Before Dy and Sandro filed their bill, the Akbayan Reform Bloc had already filed House Bill 5905, which reform advocates now treat as the serious anti‑dynasty proposal. 

HB 5905 does more than track who happens to sit together in one LGU; it defines dynasty as concentration and hand‑over of power, and tries to stop relatives up to the fourth degree from running in ways that create simultaneous control or easy succession, including in the party‑list system. 

It covers non‑traditional setups and gives COMELEC more teeth, drawing on years of research about how political families actually work on the ground.

Putting HB 6771 and HB 5905 side by side highlights two completely different roads: one bill that tries to meaningfully cut into dynastic control, and another that lets clans keep most of their machinery while allowing politicians to brag that there is finally an “anti‑dynasty law.”

🟥 Why the Lack of “Urgent” Tag Matters

The “urgent” question looks technical but says a lot about how serious this administration is. 

Article VI, Section 26(2) of the 1987 Constitution requires three readings on different days and a three‑day gap before final passage, unless the president certifies that a bill needs immediate enactment to meet a public calamity or emergency. 

Once a bill is certified urgent, Congress can approve it on second and third reading on the same day, which cuts the waiting time and forces movement.

Reformists and opposition lawmakers asked the obvious question: if Malacañang is serious about political reforms like the anti‑dynasty bill, why not use that power now? 

Labeling a measure as “priority” without the urgent tag gives Congress room to sit on it in committee, while the Palace still gets to point to a list and say, “Look, it’s there.”

Malacañang’s official line is that they do not want a rushed, half‑baked law. 

Officials repeatedly said Marcos wants time to study the bill and close loopholes, not a quick headline victory that collapses when challenged. 

Palace spokesperson Claire Castro stressed that legislation like this should not be hurried, that it needs full debate and review, and that passing a weak law just for optics would be the real political gimmick. 

Reports of the LEDAC meeting say Marcos told lawmakers to produce a solid, properly crafted version instead of something passed just so they can claim to have delivered an anti‑dynasty law.

On paper, the explanation sounds reasonable; in practice, it repeats a familiar pattern where big reforms are “studied” indefinitely while the hard parts are delayed.

🟥 The Ningas Cogon Pattern

The anti‑dynasty bill episode fits into a bigger pattern around this administration. 

Big programs start with strong messaging, but follow‑through is uneven, weak, absent. 

Early economic speeches promised lower prices and stronger agriculture, yet inflation and food supply issues have remained stubbornly present and the responses have felt fragmented. 

The Maharlika Wealth Fund was launched as a flagship sovereign fund, but by late 2025 it was still holding a large amount of non‑investable capital and was under pressure to show where and how it would actually invest.

The same script appears in the flood‑control and infrastructure scandals. 

Marcos ordered an ₱80‑billion freeze on questionable infrastructure funds and vowed charges against ghost projects, leading to talk of a DPWH shake‑up and at least one high‑profile resignation. 

The initial moves sounded decisive; the story of actual prosecutions and permanent system fixes has been slower and less visible.

The pattern starts to resemble ningas cogon: a strong start, fading intensity, and a quiet change of attention to the next big thing. 

The anti‑dynasty push—priority on paper, soft bill on the floor, “we need more study” when asked to move faster—slots neatly into that same rhythm.

🟥 The Term‑Extension Angle

This is where the idea of term extension starts to enter the picture. 

If those in power are operating under a strict, non‑negotiable single term, there is more pressure to deliver results within that window. 

When unfinished reforms pile up while talk of changing the Constitution keeps resurfacing, the incentives start to look different.

Since 2023, Marcos and his allies have repeatedly opened the door to Charter Change, often framing it as purely “economic” amendments meant to attract investment. 

He floated the idea of holding a plebiscite on charter amendments alongside the 2025 elections, and later Malacañang said he is open to constitutional change through a convention to “close loopholes.” 

In Philippine politics, phrases like that are rarely neutral; past Cha‑cha pushes have nearly always carried some attempt to tweak political rules, including term limits.

Putting these pieces together creates a different picture of what is actually being prioritized. 

A strong, real anti‑dynasty law would hurt many incumbents, including members of the current ruling network. 

Allowing a softer version to move, refusing to certify it urgent, and wrapping it in talk of “careful study” keeps the constitutional promise technically alive without seriously threatening dynastic power. 

At the same time, energy and political capital continue to circle back to constitutional change, which, if it ever succeeds, could rewrite the rules on term limits or restructure the system in ways that still favor those already in power.

In that context, the term‑extension angle starts to make political sense. 

If there is a belief that the rules can eventually be changed through Cha‑cha, the pressure to fix things within one term is weaker. 

Ending dynasties now would close off some options for the same families later. 

Keeping anti‑dynasty and accountability measures in a permanent “in progress” state keeps the reform story alive while preserving room to fight a bigger battle over the Constitution itself.

Taken together, the behavior around the anti‑dynasty bill fits that pattern: enough movement to claim reform, not enough to seriously close off the system that sustains the current political class.

🟥 Where Things Stood at the End of December

By the time Congress went on its year‑end break, the scoreboard was simple. 

The anti‑dynasty bill sat on the president’s priority list but was not certified urgent. 

HB 6771 had been filed and referred to committee. 

Stronger proposals like HB 5905 remained technically alive but untouched. Serious action was moved to 2026.

On paper, this can be described as “progress.” In practice, it looks like another case of ningas cogon: a loud start, careful hedging in the middle, and plenty of space left later to change the rules in favor of the same families the 1987 Constitution promised to limit.

🟥 Final Thoughts

This is not a case of “hindi mo lang alam ang timeline” or “masyado ka namang nagmamadali.” 

The whole point of laying out the dates, the LEDAC meeting, the filing of HB 6771, the missing urgent certification, and the promised hearings pushed to next year is to show that the calendar is very clear—and that the delay is a choice, not an accident. 

The problem is not that people are rushing; the problem is that, in the face of dynasties, corruption, and outright thievery, this administration is still comfortable moving at a pace that protects powerful families more than it protects the public. 

Any self‑respecting president who truly understood the magnitude of that damage would not hide behind “kailangan pang pag‑aralan” or “wag magmadali”; he would pull every string available to act quickly and decisively, and let the political cost fall where it needs to fall.

🟥 The Hidden Tell: What Your Questions and Comments Say About You

I always pay attention to how a person comments or asks a question. Not because I enjoy judging people, but because it quietly exposes something: did they really read, or did they just react. 

Most of the time, the way someone frames a question tells me if they’re lazy with reading, half‑read the thing, or put in zero effort at all.  

And it’s not just gut feel. Research in psychology, communication, and education all say the same thing: how we ask questions and comment is a window into how deeply we’re thinking, how much we understood, and how seriously we took the content.  

🟥 Questions Are More Than Just Questions

A question isn’t just a sentence with a question mark. It’s a signal of what your brain did before you opened your mouth (or typed your comment).  

Researchers have found that people who ask more and better questions—especially follow‑up questions—are seen as more engaged and more likeable, because their questions prove they were actually listening or reading closely. 

On the flip side, when someone asks something that’s already very clearly answered in the post, that usually signals low effort, not low IQ. They didn’t engage; they just reacted.  

There’s also solid neuroscience behind it. Once you ask a question, your brain starts working to find answers. 

The deeper the question, the deeper the mental work that happened beforehand. 

That’s why the quality of a question often reveals the quality of the thinking that led to it.  

🟥 Three Types of Reading You See in the Comments

Educational psychology talks about “cognitive engagement,” but let’s simplify. Based on research, there are roughly three modes people operate in when they consume content.  

1. Shallow readers: the skimmers  

These are the headline and first-line-only people. They scroll, skim a few lines, and jump to the comments. Studies show this style of reading—called shallow processing—focuses only on surface features like titles or bold phrases, not on meaning. The result: low retention, low understanding. Their questions? Usually things already answered in the second or third paragraph.  

2. Half-readers: the triggered ones

These people read a bit more, but they stop the moment something makes them emotional or offended. 

They latch onto one part of the text and ignore the rest. 

Research on digital reading and online discussions shows that this pattern often leads to misunderstanding, because they don’t process the full context. 

Their questions often sound like: “So you’re saying ___?” when the article explicitly clarified the opposite.  

3. Deep readers: the ones who actually finish reading the piece
 
These are the rare ones who read from start to finish, think about it, and then comment. 

Deep processing means actively connecting what you read with what you already know, asking yourself questions, and putting ideas together. 

Studies show that people who read this way remember more and understand more deeply—sometimes far more than shallow readers. 

Their questions sound like: “How does this compare to what happened in 2019?” or “Is this similar to the policy you mentioned before?”  

The content is the same. The difference is the effort—and the effort shows up in the questions.  

🟥 When Confidence Is High but Understanding Is Low

There’s a special kind of comment you see a lot online: very confident, very strong wording, but clearly based on a shallow or wrong reading of the post. 

Psychology has a name for this: the Dunning–Kruger effect.  

In simple terms, the Dunning–Kruger effect happens when people who know little about a topic think they know a lot. 

Studies found that those who perform poorly on a task tend to overestimate how well they did, while those who perform well often underestimate themselves. 

The problem? People who lack knowledge also lack the ability to see their own gaps.  

In the comments, it looks like this:  

- They attack an argument the article never actually made.  

- They dismiss experts or sources without even checking them.  

- They rely on “pakiramdam ko” instead of evidence, even in technical topics.  

Research on online misinformation and digital skills shows that people with weak digital literacy struggle to judge accuracy and are more vulnerable to false or low‑quality content. Their questions and comments often defend their pride, not pursue the truth.  

🟥 The Missing Skill: Knowing That You Didn’t Understand

Good readers don’t just read; they also watch their own understanding while reading. This skill is called "comprehension monitoring."  

Studies show that strong readers do certain things when something doesn’t make sense: they slow down, reread, look up unfamiliar terms, or adjust how they’re reading. 

Weak readers, on the other hand, often don’t notice when they’ve stopped understanding and just continue anyway.  

Researchers using eye‑tracking found that people with better comprehension are more likely to pause and go back when the text is confusing or inconsistent. 

Those who don’t do this tend to misunderstand more and perform worse in comprehension tasks.  

Online, that difference is obvious:  

- The reflective reader says, “Wait, baka ako yung hindi nakaintindi,” scrolls back up, and rereads before commenting.  

- The lazy reader says, “Di ko gets, so mali ‘to,” and leaves a hostile comment without checking if they misunderstood.  

Research on online reading strategies confirms that using strategies like rereading, summarizing, and checking understanding leads to better performance on comprehension tests. 

The people who never use these strategies? They’re the ones asking questions the text already answered.  

🟥 Not All Questions Are Created Equal

There are questions that open up thinking and questions that shut it down. Education research calls out the difference clearly.  

Dead‑end questions are based on a negative starting point and don’t invite real thinking, like:  

- “Why am I so bad at understanding this?”  
- “Bakit ang gulo nito?”  
- “Ano ba ‘to, walang kwenta?”

These questions push the brain to just confirm the negative premise instead of exploring solutions.  

Powerful questions, on the other hand, are open and curious:  

- “What am I missing here?”  
- “How does this connect to what I already know?”  
- “What evidence supports or goes against this?”  

Studies show that students who ask higher‑order questions—those that analyze, compare, and evaluate—tend to understand better and learn more deeply. They’re more engaged and better at critical thinking.  

Same thing in comments:  

- “So fake news ‘to?” is a dead‑end question.  
- “Do you have a source for this part?” is a more useful one.  
- “How does this match with [insert other event or data]?” is even better.  

🟥 Media Literacy: Why Some People Keep Getting It Wrong

In a world full of fake news, edited clips, and algorithm‑driven feeds, media literacy—how well you can evaluate what you see online—is now a survival skill.  

In large studies, people with higher digital literacy perform better at telling true headlines from false ones. 

They don’t just react; they verify. 

They are more careful about what they believe and share.  

People with stronger media literacy tend to:  

- Ask where information came from.  
- Recognize the difference between a news report, an opinion piece, and a random post.  
- Show more thoughtful civic engagement, like checking facts before sharing or commenting.  

Low media literacy, on the other hand, shows up in predictable ways:  

- Sharing content without reading past the headline.  
- Treating every post as equally credible.  
- Challenging facts that are clearly cited in the article while bringing no sources of their own.  

The pattern is consistent: the weaker the media literacy, the weaker the questions.  

🟥 Active vs Passive Learning: You Can See It in the Thread

Teachers have long differentiated between passive and active learning. 

Passive learning is when you just sit there and take it in. Active learning is when you wrestle with the ideas.  

Research shows that active learning leads to better retention, deeper understanding, and stronger critical thinking skills. In an online setting, you can almost see who’s in which mode.  

Passive engagement looks like this:  

- “Nice post.”  
- “Disagree.” (with no explanation)  
- Questions that simply ask what the text already said.  

Active engagement looks like this:  

- “You said X in paragraph three. How does that work with Y?”  
- “This reminds me of [specific event]. Is this similar or different?”  
- “I see your point, but here’s some data that suggests another angle.”  

Studies of YouTube and social media comments show that more thoughtful comments tend to include reasoning, evidence, or connections to other ideas. 

They take more time and more cognitive effort to write.  

🟥 Effort and Understanding: You Can’t Cheat This

One of the most consistent findings in reading research is almost embarrassingly simple: the more effort you put into reading, the better you understand.  

Studies show that effort acts as a bridge between confidence and actual performance. 

People who report putting in more effort when reading get better comprehension scores. 

When reading goals demand deeper understanding—for example, studying to really grasp the idea instead of just browsing—people tend to read more slowly but understand more.  

In the digital world, this basic rule gets ignored. The platforms are built for speed and reaction, not reflection. 

That’s why so many comments clearly come from people who didn’t invest any real effort in understanding what they just read.  

🟥 What Research Says About Comments in the Wild

Researchers have actually studied social media comment sections to see what separates thoughtful engagement from low‑effort noise.  

In one study, “thoughtful” comments tended to:  

- Be longer.  
- Use more verbs (indicating action and reasoning).  
- Include logical connectors like “because”, “however”, “for example”.  
- Stay relevant to the original content.  

Using these patterns, they were able to train a system to detect more thoughtful comments with fairly high accuracy. The patterns were "that" consistent.  

Other research looking at millions of comments across platforms over decades found that:  

- Longer threads are more likely to turn toxic.  
- Strong emotions and opposing views often fuel hostile exchanges.  
- Toxic comments are frequently tied to shallow engagement—not real, informed disagreement.  

So when someone angrily attacks a position the article never took, it usually means they reacted more than they read. 

Emotion first, comprehension later—or never.  

🟥 Does Education Make a Difference?

Studies also show that education level connects to how people engage with information and with comment sections.  

One study on commenters and comment readers found that:  

- People who read comments tend to be more educated than those who don’t.  
- People who both read and write comments are, on average, more educated than those who ignore comment sections entirely.  

Education itself doesn’t guarantee thoughtful comments (we’ve all seen smart people say foolish things online), but it does correlate with:  

- Better critical thinking skills.  
- Better reading comprehension and monitoring.  
- More advanced question-asking and analysis.  
- Stronger media literacy and healthier information habits.  

Those skills show up in how someone frames their question. You see more nuance, more context, more actual reasoning.  

🟥 Owning Our Habits as Readers and Commenters

At the end of the day, the point isn’t to rank people as good or bad commenters, but to face an uncomfortable truth: our comments and questions expose how we think and how much effort we’re willing to invest in understanding.

If you catch yourself:  

- Asking things that were already clearly answered.  
- Reacting to headlines instead of reading the full piece.  
- Making bold claims on topics you barely read about.  
- Dismissing evidence just because it contradicts your opinion.  

Those are red flags—not about your worth as a person, but about your habits as a reader and thinker.  

The online world is designed to keep us shallow. Fast scroll. Quick like. Instant outrage. 

But research is clear: deep engagement, critical thinking, and genuine understanding only come when we slow down, pay attention, and ask better questions.  

So next time you’re about to drop a comment or a question, pause for a second:  
- Did I actually read this?  
- Am I asking from curiosity or from ego?  
- Is my question revealing that I understood—or that I didn’t bother to?  

Because whether we like it or not, our questions and comments are always saying something about us. 

The only real question is: are we aware of what they’re revealing.

I’ve always been deeply curious about the story of Fr. Juan Severino Mallari. 

I wanted to understand how a priest could end up remembered as the first serial killer in the Philippines.

When I sit with the scattered accounts long enough, the glow of 2025 fades, and Magalang in the early 1800s comes into focus.

The town lies under the shadow of Mount Arayat, rice fields flattening out in every direction, the air heavy with mud, smoke, and the sweet-sour smell of carabao. 

At its center stands San Bartolome Church: thick stone walls, a simple façade, the kind of structure that has already survived more than one earthquake. 

Inside, candles bruise the darkness in small, wavering circles. 

A young priest bends over a desk in the rectory, his hand moving in slow, deliberate strokes across parchment.

Ink flowers bloom in the margins of parish reports—roses and vines curling around columns of names and numbers, angels perched on clouds above lists of baptisms and burials. 

His calligraphy is so precise it looks printed, each letter disciplined yet somehow alive. 

At the bottom of each page, beneath the flourishes, he signs his name: Juan Severino Mallari.

Just reaching this desk was a kind of battle. 

Born in Macabebe, he studied theology at the University of Santo Tomas, one of the first few Filipinos to step through doors meant for Spanish sons. 

He applied three times for parish assignments—Orani, Mariveles, Lubao—and three times the colonial authorities chose Spaniards instead. 

Filipino priests, they said in different words, were not ready. 

They were too provincial, too superstitious, too indio. 

When he finally secured the parish of Magalang in 1816, he became the first native priest to hold a parish in Pampanga, a single brown face in a clergy dominated by white men.

On Sundays, he moves through the liturgy with practiced grace. 

The people watch him at the altar—this priest who speaks their language and carries their features, yet wears the vestments of a power that has ruled them for centuries. 

They come to him with all the usual needs: a sick child, a cramped marriage, a bad harvest. He listens, prays, writes their names into his neat columns.

Then his mother arrives.

She comes from Macabebe to live with him, already showing signs of a strange illness. 

At first it looks like exhaustion: a little more sleep, a little less appetite. 

Then it grips harder. Fever, weakness, weight dropping off her frame, the body shrinking into itself. 

There is no clear diagnosis in the books he read in Manila, no Latin label for this kind of wasting. Doctors are few, and their remedies are limited. Herbal healers try their concoctions and fail.

Around them, the town supplies a different explanation, older than the church walls: nakulam. Someone has cursed her. 

In the stalls of the market and the corners of nipa houses, people murmur about mangkukulam—those who can send sickness across distance with herbs, prayers, and pins stuck into wax or cloth. 

Catholicism has not erased this vocabulary; it has learned to live beside it. A person can recite the Creed and still fear a curse.

It is not hard to imagine how this word lands on a mind under strain. 

Mallari is the first Filipino priest in a system that has told him, over and over, that he does not belong. 

The pressure to be perfect presses on him from all sides: from Spanish superiors waiting for him to fail, from parishioners expecting one of their own to be better than the friars, from his own ambition and insecurity. 

Now, in the close dark of his quarters, the person he loves most lies dying, and nobody can explain why.

He prays harder. He clutches the rosary until his fingers ache. In the silence between beads, he starts to hear another kind of logic: if someone cast a curse, someone can undo it. 

If witches did this, witches must be stopped. 

Saving his mother becomes not only a plea to God but a task, a mission that begins to form in the cracks of his sanity.

The first body appears sometime after 1816. 

The records will not give the victim’s name—only that people in Magalang began to disappear or to turn up dead without clear cause. 

A farmer found in a field. 
A woman who doesn’t make it home. 
A laborer whose death no one can quite explain.

 Each case looks, at first glance, like an isolated tragedy in a province where hardship is normal and native deaths are low priorities for colonial officials.

He continues to say Mass.

In the rectory, people keep coming. 

A man wanting counsel about a family dispute; a woman seeking prayers for a sick child; a parishioner with a small favor to ask. They enter the priest’s private space, trusting the collar and the gentle voice they hear every Sunday. 

Behind that door, something else is happening. 

In the fractured map of his mind, they are not neighbors but suspects, not sheep to be shepherded but possible sources of the curse eating his mother alive.

The exact details of each killing are lost. There is no surviving transcript describing how he chose, what he said, how he moved. 

What remains are signs. 

Over the years, more townspeople vanish. Some are found dead with no obvious motive or thief to blame. 

The pattern is invisible to those looking only at the surface. It will only become clear when someone finally sees what he has been hiding.

His mother dies on December 4, 1825. 

Whatever bargain he believed he was keeping, whatever ritual he thought he was fulfilling, fails in the most obvious way. Her body cools. Her breath stops. The curse, if there ever was one, has run its course.

The killings do not stop.

By this point, whatever line once separated his grief from his delusion has blurred beyond recovery. He has been living for years in a mental world where invisible enemies surround him, where each death is not murder but necessary medicine. 

Even without his original motive, the logic of that world holds. The idea of witches does not vanish with his mother’s last breath. It has sunk too deep.

In December 1826, Mallari falls ill himself. 

He can no longer perform all his duties, and another priest is sent to assist and, if needed, take over the running of the parish. 

This newcomer walks into the same stone rectory, smells the same faint mix of candle wax and old paper, and begins to learn the daily rhythms of the parish. 

He also begins to open drawers and chests in search of linens, vestments, anything required for the sacraments.

Somewhere in that search, a lid lifts on the hidden evidence.

Inside a chest or cabinet—again, the specifics are not preserved—he finds clothes. 

Many clothes. 

Men’s shirts, women’s skirts, children’s garments. 

They are neatly folded, but the neatness cannot hide the stains: brown-black patches where blood dried long ago. 

They are not his. They do not belong to any living resident of the rectory. They belong to people who have gone missing, to bodies found without explanation, to families who never received these last traces back.

This is the point where the story stops being rumor and becomes a case.

Authorities are notified. 
Soldiers arrive. 
Mallari is arrested, taken from the rectory where his mother once lay and his ink once dried. 

In questioning, according to later accounts, he does not deny what he has done. Instead, he explains. These were witches. They had cursed his mother. He killed them to break the spell. He believes, or at least once believed, that he was fighting evil with evil, blood with blood.

For the Spanish colonial authorities, his confession is useful. 

It confirms what they have long whispered: native priests are unstable, too close to the “superstitions” of the people, unfit for the responsibilities they crave. 

In a Church already torn by tensions over secularization—the question of whether Filipino secular clergy should be given more parishes instead of the powerful religious orders—Mallari’s case becomes an argument in flesh and rope for keeping Filipinos out.

The law, such as it is, moves slowly. Mallari is taken to Manila and imprisoned. 

Fourteen years pass between his arrest and his execution. 

In those years, another institution stands across the city: Hospicio de San Jose, an island refuge on the Pasig (side note: the river, not the place) where orphans, the elderly, and the mentally ill are taken in and cared for by religious sisters. 

The place is hardly a modern psychiatric hospital, but it represents a different possibility: that those whose minds have broken might be contained and tended rather than displayed and killed.

Mallari does not go there. He remains in prison, his case winding through a colonial legal system more interested in example than in understanding. 

Whatever diagnosis a modern psychiatrist might give—psychosis, severe mental disorder—will only be applied retroactively by later historians. At the time, there is only crime and punishment.

By 1840, the long wait ends.

Bagumbayan, the wide field outside the walls of Intramuros, is bare under the Manila sky. It serves as a military parade ground, a public space, and a stage for executions. 

The gallows are visible from a distance. People gather, some out of morbid curiosity, some because any public spectacle breaks the monotony of colonial life.

Guards bring him out. He has been in custody for more than a decade. His hair, his face, his posture are all altered by years in confinement that the records do not bother to describe. They climb the scaffold. 

One Filipino priest about to be hanged for murdering dozens of his own people.

No document records his final thoughts.

No scribe writes down his last words in detail. 

The rope is placed around his neck. 

Whatever he believed about witches, curses, his mother, and God must resolve into something in that instant, but history keeps it to itself. 

The trap gives way. 

The crowd watches. 

The colonial authorities show that even a priest can hang.

Decades later, Bagumbayan will claim other clerical bodies: Fathers Gomez, Burgos, and Zamora, executed in 1872 after a rebellion they did not lead, their deaths fueling a secularization movement and nationalist fire. 

José Rizal will fall there in 1896, facing a firing squad with his back turned. 

Their names are etched into monuments and textbooks. Mallari’s name is mostly an oddity in crime lists and horror articles.

His victims, estimated at fifty-seven, remain largely faceless and nameless in the surviving accounts. 

Their families do not appear in the official record. 

The number itself is repeated so often it feels solid, yet when traced back it rests on thin documentation, on partial reports and later reconstructions. 

What is certain is that people died, that they were parishioners who trusted their priest, and that their stories were never given the weight his has received.

Today, San Bartolome Church still stands in Magalang. 

Its stones have been repaired and repainted. Its bells still ring over the town. 

Families gather there for Mass and weddings and funerals, most of them not thinking about the first Filipino priest who once wrote their ancestors’ names in elegant script and hid their murdered neighbors’ clothes in his quarters. 

If you sit quietly in a pew long enough, the building feels more like a survivor than a haunted house—a place that has watched generations pass and kept its own counsel.

The story of Fr. Juan Severino Mallari hangs between categories that modern readers like to keep separate. 

He is a killer, yes, a man who took lives in numbers that stagger for a rural parish. 

He is also, by all reasonable inferences, a man whose mind broke under the combined weight of personal grief, cultural beliefs about curses, and a colonial system that gave him responsibility but little support and no language for mental illness. 

He is a priest elevated as an exception and then paraded as a warning.

There is no neat moral to extract. No redemption arc, no clean condemnation that does justice to both the horror of what he did and the context that shaped him. 

What we have instead are fragments: calligraphed reports, later testimonies, numbers without names, a stretch of ground in Manila where his body once swung, a church in Pampanga that goes on ringing its bells.

The rest happens in the space between the reader and the page, where imagination tries to fill the gaps that history left behind.

Dear "religious" groups on Facebook,

Please stop using AI-generated videos and photos to promote anything religious. It does not work the way you think it does, and this is not just a matter of taste but of trust, authenticity, and basic honesty. 

🟥 We recognize AI content now  

It is 2025. People already know what generic AI and “ChatGPT-style” content looks and feels like, both in text and visuals.  

The tone, the plastic faces, the over-perfect light, the cliché captions – your audience has seen this pattern over and over again and they can smell it from a mile away.  

Instead of encountering something sacred, they feel like they have stumbled into yet another bland, mass-produced content mill dressed up in pious language. 

🟥 AI visuals damage trust  

Once people suspect something is synthetic, their trust in the message and the messenger goes down.  

Many ordinary users now expect transparency if an image is AI-generated and feel less positive toward organizations that use artificial visuals without being upfront about it.  

When you use obviously fake religious images and animations, you may think you are “enhancing” the Gospel, but what many people actually see is one more attention-hacking trick in a feed already full of scams and spam. 

🟥 Religion for clout and cash  

There is also an uncomfortable reality here: many religious Facebook pages are not really about faith; they are about clout and algorithmic income.  

The goal is engagement, ad revenue, and monetized reach, with religion used as a convenient theme to trigger shares, comments, and emotional reactions that feed the platform’s ad system.  

The result is a flood of low-effort AI images of Jesus, Mary, angels, and “miracles” packaged with manipulative captions, designed more for click-throughs than for conversion of heart. 

Personally, I am very much aware of this pattern, and I actively block these pages because the majority of them are garbage.  

They don’t deepen faith, they don’t form conscience, and they don’t build community; they merely game the algorithm under a religious label. 

🟥 AI cannot carry sacred meaning  

Generative systems do not understand holiness or dignity; they remix patterns from whatever data they were trained on, including biased, shallow, or disrespectful material about different faiths.  

When you ask them to “create” Jesus, Mary, or a heavenly vision, they are not praying, contemplating, or witnessing; they are statistically guessing pixels.  

That is the opposite of what sacred images are for, which is why AI-generated iconography often feels eerie, kitschy, or flat-out wrong, even to people who can’t explain why in words. 

🟥 If you care about faith, stop  

If you genuinely care about faith and not just reach, this is the bottom line: AI-generated religious images and videos are hurting, not helping.  

They undermine trust, cheapen devotion, and blend the sacred into the same artificial noise people are already trying to escape from in their feeds. 

Use AI for things it is good at – drafting notes, organizing ideas, maybe illustrating clearly non-devotional concepts – but do not turn the heart of your faith into content filler for an algorithm.  

If what you are posting is truly about God, then let it be rooted in real people, real communities, real stories, real art – not in synthetic faces designed to farm clicks.

I saw a post about Sara Duterte supposedly backing nearly P1 billion worth of DPWH projects in 2020 under her father’s presidency. 

I’d seen the claim before in passing, but I realized I didn’t actually know the details, if there were any. So I looked into it.

And the more I read, the clearer the pattern looked.

🟥 What the leaked files say

Multiple reports and “DPWH leaks” say Sara Duterte was listed as a proponent for 16 DPWH line items in the 2020 budget.  

Those 16 line items reportedly totaled nearly P1 billion, all classified as road projects.  

Fifteen of them were in Davao City, and one was in Davao del Sur, during the time she was mayor and her father, Rodrigo Duterte, was president.  

Two projects are described as being tagged under “Mayor Sara Z. Duterte / RD Allan Borromeo, Region XI,” tying the proposals to the local chief executive and the regional DPWH office.  

Her brother Paolo Duterte also appears in the same sets of files, reportedly linked to 16 line items worth about P764.6 million, fourteen of which supposedly made it into the final 2020 budget.  

These details come from media write-ups and social media posts that claim to be based on internal DPWH records and the so‑called “Cabral files,” but the full original internal files are not publicly accessible yet, and the claims have not gone through a completed court process.  

🟥  How this connects to the 2020 budget

To understand why “P1 billion in 2020” matters, you have to place it in the national budget process.  

The executive branch first prepares the National Expenditure Program (NEP), which is the President’s proposed budget submitted to Congress.  

Congress then debates, amends, and adds “insertions” or “amendments” during committee hearings, plenary debates, and the bicameral conference, before approving the final General Appropriations Act (GAA), which becomes the budget law.  

What makes the current controversy sensitive is that former DPWH officials and recent explainers say some politicians’ projects were already reflected in the NEP itself, meaning they were accommodated during the executive drafting stage, not just later in Congress.  

Reports on the “DPWH leaks” imply that certain Sara Duterte–linked projects were present in the official 2020 GAA, suggesting they survived the entire process from NEP to final law, although the exact project list and physical status on the ground have not been fully audited publicly.  

So the story is not just “there were projects,” but “these projects were allegedly prioritized and protected inside a system already known for insertions and political bargaining.”  

🟥 The wider flood control and DPWH scandal

All of this is coming out in the middle of a much bigger DPWH corruption scandal centered on flood control and infrastructure projects nationwide.  

Timelines describe allegations of ghost projects, overpriced contracts, rigged bidding, and kickbacks involving contractors, DPWH officials, and politicians over many years.  

Maria Catalina Cabral, the former DPWH undersecretary for planning, became a central figure because she oversaw project programming and reportedly kept detailed digital records of who requested what, and when.  

Before her death, DPWH turned over about ten years’ worth of her files to the Office of the Ombudsman, and a congressman separately released portions related to the 2025 budget, showing long lists of proponents and allocations.  

Media and some politicians now refer to leaked or shared extracts from these records as the “DPWH leaks,” which allegedly include entries for senators, representatives, local officials, and personalities linked to both the Marcos and Duterte camps.  

Within that context, Sara Duterte’s reported P1 billion in 2020 DPWH projects is one piece of a much larger pattern of potential abuse of public funds through infrastructure budgeting.  

🟥 What Sara Duterte has said publicly

Sara Duterte has repeatedly denied involvement in the ongoing flood control scam.  

She has emphasized that the Office of the Vice President and the Department of Education, which she previously headed, did not implement DPWH projects and did not have DPWH flood control funds under her control.  

She has also dismissed some allegations as “noise” meant to damage her and prevent a possible future presidential run.  

On the broader flood control hearings, she described the investigations as a kind of “zarzuela,” saying that if the President truly wanted to uncover everything, he could supposedly do it in one day.  

Up to now, there has been no final court ruling specifically declaring Sara Duterte criminally liable over the 2020 DPWH projects; what exists are leaked or publicized lists, media analysis, and political commentary that raise serious questions but are still in the stage of investigation and public debate.  

🟥 The Davao pattern and dynasty politics

The P1 billion figure becomes more alarming when you zoom out to the broader pattern of infrastructure allocations to the Duterte family during Rodrigo Duterte’s presidency.  

DPWH and later media reports confirmed that Representative Paolo Duterte’s district in Davao City received about P51 billion in infrastructure funds from 2020 to 2022, an amount described as unusually large compared to other districts.  

In several of those years, the proposed NEP amounts for his district were much lower than what ended up in the final GAA, showing huge jumps after congressional and executive adjustments.  

Civil society groups and local critics have challenged the Duterte family to show transparent documentation and physical proof of all these projects, arguing that Davao’s already high level of development did not justify such disproportionate allocations while many poorer areas remained underfunded.  

When you place Sara’s alleged P1 billion in 2020 road projects beside Paolo’s P51 billion over three years, the picture that emerges is not just about one person’s projects, but about how political dynasties can channel national resources toward their strongholds while holding national power.  

Even if some or many of these projects are physically real, the questions of fairness, prioritization, and possible kickbacks remain, especially in light of the ongoing DPWH corruption investigations.  

🟥 What is clear, and what isn’t

So, what can be said clearly at this point?

- Multiple reports, citing DPWH‑related files, claim that Sara Duterte was listed as proponent for 16 DPWH road projects worth almost P1 billion in the 2020 budget, mostly in Davao City.  
- These claims fit within a broader pattern of unusually high infrastructure allocations to the Duterte family’s bailiwicks during Rodrigo Duterte’s presidency, including the confirmed P51 billion for Paolo Duterte’s district from 2020–2022.  
- The “Cabral files” and “DPWH leaks” have triggered investigations into how insertions and allocations were made at both the NEP and GAA stages, raising questions about the role of powerful politicians and possible corruption.  
- As of now, there is no final court decision specifically convicting Sara Duterte for graft related to these 2020 DPWH projects, and she denies any wrongdoing.  

What is not clear yet:

- The full, official project list tied to her name, as validated by all relevant government agencies and investigators.  
- The status and quality of each project on the ground, including whether any were ghost projects, overpriced, or used as channels for kickbacks.  
- How investigators, the Ombudsman, and possibly the courts will ultimately assess liability, if any, on her part.  

For now, the most honest way to put it is this: there is serious documentary smoke around Sara Duterte’s role in almost P1 billion worth of DPWH projects in 2020, within a system already riddled with confirmed abuse, but the legal fire — formal findings, convictions, accountability — is still catching up.
