🟥 When the Watchdogs Walk Away: What Three Resignations Tell Us About Accountability

There's this pattern you start to notice when you've been watching Philippine politics long enough. 

Someone promises accountability. 
The public gets angry enough that something has to be done. 
A commission is formed. 
Headlines are made. 
And then, quietly, the people tasked with finding the truth start walking away.

Rossana Fajardo just became the third official to resign from the Independent Commission for Infrastructure. She's out by December 31. 

Before her, Rogelio Singson quit on December 15, citing health issues and security concerns. And back in September, Benjamin Magalong stepped down as special adviser, raising questions about the commission's independence.

Three people. Three months. One commission that's supposed to investigate the biggest infrastructure corruption scandal in recent Philippine history.

Now we're left with basically one man standing, Chairman Andres Reyes, trying to investigate a P545 billion corruption scheme.

Let that sink in.

🟥 The Promise That Wasn't

President Marcos said it clearly: those involved in the flood control mess would be jailed before Christmas. "They will not have a Merry Christmas," he said in November. The deadline was specific. The promise was public.

Christmas came and went.

Know who spent the holidays behind bars? Contractors Sarah and Curlee Discaya. Nine DPWH engineers. District-level officials. The usual small fish.

And the big fish? The ones with the power to insert billions into the budget, the ones who allegedly collected 25–30% kickbacks? 

Senators Joel Villanueva and Jinggoy Estrada, implicated by multiple DPWH officials who say they personally delivered cash to their staff. 

Senator Francis Escudero, who faces scrutiny over an P800 million money trail. 

Former Senator Bong Revilla. 

Former Representative Zaldy Co, now a fugitive hiding somewhere in Europe.

All still free.

The Palace now says to wait until 2026. More arrests are coming, they promise. It's only been four months, they say. The Napoles case took a year before anyone was jailed, they remind us.

And that's supposed to reassure us?

🟥 The Anatomy of a Failing Investigation

Let's talk about why the ICI is collapsing.

First: it has no teeth.

The ICI can investigate. It can subpoena. It can gather evidence and hold hearings. 

What it cannot do is prosecute anyone. 

It can only recommend cases to the Ombudsman, who then decides whether to file charges at the Sandiganbayan.

That's not an independent investigation. That's an expensive fact-finding mission.

Second: it has no money.

Singson said it plainly before he resigned: they had not received any funds. Even their printer was borrowed. They lacked personnel and had no funds for salaries. Staff were basically volunteers.

How do you investigate a P545 billion corruption scheme with borrowed printers and volunteer staff?

Third: it has no real protection.

Singson cited security concerns when he resigned. He said he was risking his life and his family's safety "to resolve Malacañang's problems." When the people investigating corruption are afraid for their lives, and the government can't or won't protect them, what message does that send?

Fourth: it has no political will behind it.

Congress has bills pending that would create a permanent Independent Commission Against Infrastructure Corruption with actual prosecutorial powers. These bills have been sitting in committee for months. Marcos hasn't certified them as urgent. The Speaker promised they'd pass before Congress adjourned in December.

They didn't.

When your own investigators are resigning and begging for the legal tools to do their jobs, and you can't even fast-track the legislation, what does that tell you about how serious you are?

🟥 The Senators Who Can't Be Touched

Here's where it gets infuriating.

The Senate held hearings. DPWH officials testified under oath. A district engineer admitted to personally delivering hundreds of millions in cash kickbacks to a senator's staff at a rest house in Bulacan. Other engineers corroborated his testimony.

Those engineers? Arrested. Facing malversation and graft charges.

The senators? Still sitting in the Senate.

Why?

Inter-parliamentary courtesy.

It's this unwritten rule that says the Senate can't subpoena members of the House, and the House can't subpoena senators. Senator Erwin Tulfo explained it: hindi puwedeng imbestigahan ng Senado ang Kongreso, at ang Kongreso hindi rin puwedeng imbestigahan ang senador.

So when senators are accused of taking kickbacks, the Senate investigating them has to rely on their colleagues showing up voluntarily. And when they don't, there's nothing the Senate can do about it without violating this "courtesy."

Courtesy.

That's what we call the mechanism that shields senators from accountability. Courtesy.

Meanwhile, the engineers who delivered the money face life in prison.

🟥 The Ombudsman's Careful Dance

Ombudsman Jesus Crispin Remulla keeps saying charges against senators are coming. "Possible. Maybe in the next two weeks," he said at one point. Asked again if senators would be arrested by Christmas, he said charges were "possible."

But here's what he also said: plunder is overrated. Through all these years, there has been only one plunder conviction that really stuck. That's why they are looking at other crimes that are easier to prove.

Translation: even when there is evidence, they're looking for charges that don't set the bar so high that convictions become impossible.

And that tells you everything about how the Philippine justice system handles high-ranking officials.

The Sandiganbayan, the anti-graft court, has incredibly strict evidentiary standards.

You need proof beyond reasonable doubt, and when it comes to senators and congressmen, that bar gets set so high it's practically unreachable unless someone makes an obvious mistake.

That's why Janet Lim-Napoles, the mastermind of the P10 billion pork barrel scam, took years to convict. 

She's now serving a long sentence that, under Philippine law, caps at 40 years. It took over a decade from when the scandal first exploded.

And she wasn't even a senator. She was a private contractor.

🟥 The Math of Betrayal

Let me put this in numbers so you can see the scale.

Money allocated: P545 billion for flood control projects.

Economic loss from corruption: around P118.5 billion in just a couple of years.

Ghost projects identified: over 400.

People arrested: a couple of dozen as of Christmas.

Senators arrested: 0.

Congressmen arrested: 0.

Contractors arrested: 2, the Discayas.

DPWH officials arrested: around 20.

Individuals implicated in ICI's first referral to the Ombudsman: several dozen, including senators and congressmen.

Cases filed by the Ombudsman so far: only a handful.

ICI commissioners remaining: effectively 1 out of 3.

The people who approved the ghost projects are in jail. The people who delivered the kickbacks are in jail. The people who took the kickbacks? Most of them are still sitting in their Senate offices.

🟥 What Fajardo's Resignation Really Means

Fajardo's resignation letter was diplomatic. She said she had completed the work she set out to do. She said investigative responsibilities should now transfer to the Department of Justice and the Ombudsman. She expressed faith that a permanent commission with enhanced powers would be more effective.

Read between the lines.

She's saying: I’ve done what I can with the tools I was given. I’ve sent the evidence to the agencies that can actually prosecute. Now it's up to them. And if you want real accountability, you need to create a commission with actual power, not this toothless body I've been working with.

Singson was more blunt. He said the ICI was frustrated because it had no funds, no personnel, no resources. He questioned why he should risk his life and family to "resolve Malacañang's problems."

Magalong never gave a full public explanation, but his resignation came right after questions arose about the ICI's independence.

These aren't just bureaucrats moving to their next assignment. These are people walking away from a job they were supposedly appointed to do because they've seen how this ends.

The ICI has submitted referrals to the Ombudsman involving nearly a hundred individuals, including senators, congressmen, contractors, DPWH officials, even a COA commissioner. A small portion has turned into actual cases in court. A handful of people have been arrested.

And now the investigators are leaving.

🟥 The Flood That Exposed Everything

Here's the cruel irony: we only care about this because people died.

Typhoon season hit. Communities flooded. The dikes that were supposed to protect them didn't exist. The slope protection projects were never built. The drainage systems were ghost entries in government ledgers.

People in Cagayan Valley watched their homes disappear underwater and asked where the billions in flood control money went. People in Bulacan demanded investigations into why projects marked "completed" were never actually constructed.

And then the truth started coming out. A small group of contractors cornered a huge chunk of flood control contracts. Companies owned by the families of congressmen won billions in bids. Budget insertions were made in bicameral conference committees where the public couldn't see. Kickbacks of 25–30% were standard.

P118.5 billion lost to corruption in just two years. That could have created hundreds of thousands of jobs.

Instead, it lined the pockets of senators, congressmen, contractors, and officials who knew the public would be too busy surviving the next typhoon to demand accountability.

🟥 The Pattern We Keep Ignoring

This is not new. This is not unique. This is how corruption works in the Philippines.

The pork barrel scam took years to prosecute. The Marcos family's ill-gotten wealth cases took decades. Gloria Macapagal-Arroyo’s plunder cases dragged on. Joseph Estrada was convicted of plunder and then pardoned.

The pattern is always the same:

1. Scandal breaks.  
2. Public outrage.  
3. Investigations begin.  
4. Small fish get arrested.  
5. Big fish maneuver.  
6. Years pass.  
7. Public forgets.  
8. Big fish either get acquitted on technicalities or flee the country.

We've seen this movie before. We know how it ends.

The only question is whether we'll keep watching or finally demand a different script.

🟥 What This Says About Us

Marcos positioned himself as the anti-corruption president. He exposed the flood control mess during his SONA. He created the ICI. He ordered arrests. He promised accountability.

But when the investigators need resources, he doesn't fund them. When they need legal powers, he doesn't certify the legislation as urgent. When they resign citing security concerns and lack of support, he thanks them for their service and moves on.

This is what performative accountability looks like.

It's loud. It generates headlines. It produces some arrests, just enough to say something is being done. But it never quite reaches the people with real power.

Because holding senators accountable means taking on colleagues. Prosecuting congressmen means confronting members of your coalition. Going after the masterminds means admitting the rot goes all the way to the top of the system you're part of.

And that's the calculation every Philippine president makes: how much corruption can I expose without destabilizing my own position?

Marcos chose his line. Small fish, yes. Big fish, we'll see. Maybe next year. Maybe after more investigations. Maybe after the public cools down.

🟥 The Flood We're Still Ignoring

While we wait for senators to be arrested, the next budget is being crafted. The same bicameral conference committees are meeting. The same insertion processes are happening. The same contractors are positioning themselves for the next round of projects.

Because here's what no one wants to admit: this isn't about individual bad actors. This is about a system designed to enable exactly this kind of corruption.

When legislators control budget insertions with no transparency, when contractors with family ties to officials win bids, when "inter-parliamentary courtesy" shields lawmakers from investigation, when oversight bodies lack funding and legal powers, when prosecutions take years and convictions are rare, this is what you get.

Not a broken system. A system working exactly as designed.

For the people with power.

🟥 The Question No One's Asking

Here's what I want to know: if the people investigating corruption keep resigning, what does that tell the rest of us?

It tells us the fight is harder than we thought. The obstacles are bigger than we imagined. The political will is weaker than promised.

It tells us that three educated, accomplished professionals, a former DPWH secretary, a former police chief, a forensic auditor, looked at what they were being asked to do and decided it wasn't worth it.

Not because the work was too difficult. But because the tools were insufficient, the support was lacking, and the commitment was questionable.

When watchdogs walk away, it's not surrender. It's a message.

The question is whether anyone's listening.

There are broken promises, and then there are the kind that feel like a slap in the face.

The kind where a president looks you in the eye through a camera and says, “Wala silang Merry Christmas.” The kind where he promises that the big fish, the powerful senators and congressmen who stole billions meant to save us from drowning, would be in jail by December 25th.

Christmas came and went.

Not a single politician spent it behind bars.

🟥 The Promise That Wasn't

Here’s what I mean.

On November 13, 2024, President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. stood before the media and made a declaration. He said that before Christmas, many of the 37 powerful individuals implicated in the flood control scandal would have their cases completed. They would be locked up. They would not have a Merry Christmas.

“We don’t file cases for optics,” he said. “We file cases to put people in jail.”

I remember watching that press conference. I remember the firmness in his voice. The way he said the names: senators, congressmen, wealthy contractors. People who had turned disaster relief into a personal ATM.

I wanted to believe him.

There’s this thing Filipinos do, you know. We give people the benefit of the doubt even when our instincts are screaming otherwise. Pakikisama, they call it. The art of getting along. The willingness to hope that this time, maybe this time, someone in power will actually do what they said they’d do.

Christmas Eve came. No politicians arrested.

Christmas Day arrived. Still nothing.

By December 26th, the palace was already moving goalposts. More arrests coming in the new year, they said. Give investigators more time, they pleaded.

Walang nahuli. Or more accurately, walang malalaking isda na nahuli.

🟥 Who Actually Got Arrested

Let’s talk about who did end up in custody before Christmas, because this list tells you everything you need to know about how power protects power in this country.

Sarah Discaya. A contractor. The woman they’re calling the “queen of flood control,” arrested on December 17th for a P96.5 million ghost project. Ghost, meaning the government paid for it, but it was never built. The money just disappeared into someone’s pocket while communities drowned.

Maria Roma Rimando, president of St. Timothy Construction. Eight DPWH officials from Davao Occidental. Seven more from Oriental Mindoro.

Engineers. Contractors. District-level officials.

Small fish. Every single one of them.

And yes, these people should be in jail if they’re guilty. Corruption at any level is corruption. But let’s not pretend that a district engineer pocketing millions is the same as a senator or congressman who allegedly received fat kickbacks on billions of pesos worth of projects.

It’s not the same. And treating it as the same is insulting.

🟥 The Big Fish Still Swimming

You want to know who was named in this whole mess?

Senator Jinggoy Estrada. Senator Joel Villanueva. Former senators Nancy Binay, Grace Poe, and Chiz Escudero. Senator Mark Villar, who also served as DPWH secretary, the very agency at the center of this disaster.

Former House Speaker Martin Romualdez. Yes, the president’s own cousin.

Zaldy Co, the former House appropriations chair who fled the country and is now a fugitive.

A Commission on Audit commissioner. A former DPWH secretary.

These are the names that came out during months of Senate hearings. These are the people that whistleblowers and former officials pointed to. These are the individuals who allegedly controlled which projects got funded, which contractors got the contracts, and who got their cut.

Where are they?

Not in jail. Not even close.

Some are still sitting in the Senate, presumably voting on next year’s budget. Some resigned and walked away quietly. One is hiding overseas, posting videos on social media.

And the president who promised they’d be locked up by Christmas? He’s telling us to be patient.

🟥 The Scale of This Betrayal

I need you to feel how massive this is. Not just the broken promise, though that’s bad enough. I’m talking about the scale of the corruption itself.

Hundreds of billions of pesos were allocated for flood control in just the first three years of the Marcos administration. A huge slice of that is suspected to have been lost to corruption.

Hundreds of “ghost projects” were fully funded on paper but never built in real life. No dikes. No drainage. No strengthened riverbanks. Just signatures, documents, and missing money.

When typhoons hit, people died because flood control systems that were supposed to be there didn’t exist. Dams that should have been reinforced crumbled. Waterways that were supposed to be cleared were still clogged.

People died because someone wanted a new luxury car. Because someone wanted a private jet. Because someone needed to fund their next political campaign.

The contractors who got arrested? They went from near-zero revenues to billions in contracts in just a few years. That kind of growth doesn’t come from “hard work” or “good strategy.” It comes from having the right padrinos and being willing to play the game.

🟥 The Pattern I Keep Seeing

This is the part that makes my blood boil.

It’s not new. None of this is new.

The pattern is always the same. A scandal breaks. There’s public outrage. Massive protests, like the Trillion Peso March rallies where tens of thousands of Filipinos took to the streets demanding accountability. Church leaders speak out. Civil society groups mobilize.

The president makes promises. Strong promises. Emotional promises. Promises with timelines.

Then the timeline passes.

And suddenly it’s about “due process.” It’s about “building strong cases.” It’s about how we need to give investigators time.

Meanwhile, the people who actually got rich off this corruption are still living in their mansions, still driving their luxury cars, still planning their political comebacks.

An opposition lawmaker said it plainly: you can’t just target the low-level workers while the masterminds and their patrons remain untouched.

But that’s exactly what’s happening.

The engineers who signed off on ghost projects? Arrested. The contractor who built substandard dikes? Arrested. The senator who allegedly received hundreds of millions in kickbacks? Still free.

The House leader who allegedly orchestrated massive budget insertions? Resigned, but walking around like nothing happened.

This isn’t justice. This is theater.

🟥 What Marcos Actually Said vs What Happened

Let’s spell this out, because I’m tired of people pretending we misheard or misunderstood.

On November 13, 2024, Marcos said, “Alam ko, bago mag-Pasko, marami dito sa napangalanan… matatapos na ‘yung kaso nila, buo na ‘yung kaso. Makukulong na sila. Wala silang Merry Christmas.”

That’s not vague. That’s not “we will pursue justice.” That’s a deadline. A clear, specific promise.

December 25th came. Not a single one of the high-profile politicians he had allowed to be named was in jail.

Progressive lawmakers called it “the biggest Christmas scam.” They said the promise was designed to calm public anger, not to deliver real accountability.

They’re right.

On Christmas Day, while families tried to celebrate despite still recovering from floods and typhoons, opposition representatives were asking the question most of us were thinking: where are the big fish?

The palace’s response? Give us more time. Look at the asset freezes. Look at the arrests we did make.

But asset freezes are not jail time. And arresting engineers while senators walk free is not the kind of justice that was promised.

🟥 The Human Cost

Sometimes the numbers get so big that they stop feeling real. Billions this, billions that. So let’s bring it back to the ground.

A mother in Bulacan walking her 7-year-old through dirty floodwater just to get him to school, in an area that supposedly received billions for flood control.

Families in Cebu waking up to chest-deep water after a typhoon, only to learn that billions were allocated for projects that “should have been operational” when the storm came.

Communities forced to evacuate again and again, living with the constant fear that the next downpour could be the one that takes everything.

That’s what corruption looks like on the ground. It’s not just white-collar crime and fancy bank accounts. It’s homes destroyed and lives lost. It’s kids getting sick from dirty water. It’s the poor paying in blood for someone else’s stolen money.

Every peso stolen is a life put at risk.

So when Marcos promised to jail the people responsible and then failed to deliver, he didn’t just break a political promise. He told every Filipino who lost someone, who lost everything, that their suffering was negotiable.

🟥 The Excuse Machine

I’ve been watching the excuses roll in, and honestly, nakakapagod.

“We need to build solid cases.” Sure. Do that. Nobody is asking for sloppy work. But investigations started months before the promise. You don’t set a Christmas deadline if you’re not actually ready.

“We’re following due process.” Good. We should. But due process for powerful politicians shouldn’t magically move slower than due process for district engineers.

“Some suspects fled the country.” Then why weren’t they stopped? Why were people with pending corruption issues allowed to fly out while the rest of us are told to just trust the system?

“We’ve frozen billions in assets.” Fine. That’s better than nothing. But this isn’t just about frozen bank accounts. The promise was jail. The promise was “they will not have a Merry Christmas.”

We heard the words. We remember them.

🟥 What This Says About Us

This part is heavy, but it needs to be said.

We let them do this. Not intentionally, not because we approve, but because the system is designed to exhaust us.

We’re tired. We’re trying to survive. We’re hustling, paying overpriced food, worrying about rent, tuition, medical bills. Outrage becomes a luxury.

And they know that.

They know that by the time the deadline passes, most people are busy with noche buena, reunion, gift-giving. News fatigue kicks in. Life goes on.

But something changed with those big rallies. People are connecting the dots between Manila corruption and the floodwater in their own barangay. Between a senator’s kickback and their child’s coffin.

Once you see that connection, ang hirap nang magbulag-bulagan.

Marcos made that Christmas promise because he felt pressure. The moment that pressure fades, the promise fades with it.

If we want the big fish in jail, it can’t just be a seasonal outrage. It has to be sustained. Consistent. Relentless.

🟥 The Marcos Name and Corruption

Let’s be realistic here. The surname Marcos doesn’t exist in a vacuum.

The father was accused of looting billions during his time in power. Money that still hasn’t been fully traced or recovered. Money that could have built hospitals, schools, flood protection.

So when the son comes into office and talks tough on corruption, it carries a different weight. It’s not just a campaign line; it’s a test.

Will you break the pattern, or will you repeat it with better PR?

Seeing his cousin implicated in the same flood control mess and still not behind bars sends a very loud answer.

I’m not saying he is his father. But I am saying the bar for proving you’re different is much higher when your own family name is tied to the biggest corruption story in our history.

Right now, that bar is not being cleared.

🟥 What Happens Now

Christmas is over. The deadline passed. The big fish are still swimming.

So what now?

The palace says more arrests are coming. They say more cases will be filed. They say the process takes time.

Maybe it’s true. Maybe in a few months we’ll actually see senators and congressmen lining up in court, facing real charges, not just press releases.

I would love to be wrong about my skepticism.

But we’ve seen this cycle too many times. The promises. The partial moves. The focus on small players. The quiet deals. The slow fade.

Meanwhile, the same lawmakers implicated in this fiasco are still part of the budget process. The same system that allowed ghost projects and overpriced contracts is still operating.

Nothing in the structure has really changed.

🟥 My Ask

If you’re reading this and you’re as angry as I am, don’t let it fade with the holidays.

Remember that Marcos promised big fish in jail by Christmas, and we got contractors and engineers instead.

Remember this when they ask for your vote. When they present themselves as reformers. When they talk about “good governance” and “transparent leadership.”

Ask your district representative where the flood control money went. Ask them why the names we all heard in the hearings are still free. Ask them what they personally did to push for real cases, not just headlines.

Don’t let them hide behind “ongoing investigation.” Don’t let them drown you in legal jargon.

The core question is simple: if money was stolen, who took it, and why are they not in jail?

🟥 What I Believe

I believe Marcos knew what he was doing when he set that Christmas deadline.

I believe he thought a strong, dramatic promise would buy time. That people would cling to the hope of watching powerful people finally face consequences.

I believe he’s hoping we’ll forget. That by the time 2028 comes around, this will be just another footnote in a long list of scandals.

I’m holding on to the hope that he miscalculated.

I think something is different now. I think more and more Filipinos see the straight line between corruption and catastrophe. Between budget insertions and body bags. Between ghost projects and real graves.

Marcos promised us big fish for Christmas.

What we got was excuses.

And I’m not going to quietly move on and treat it like nothing happened.

Wala silang binigay na Merry Christmas sa mga nabaha, sa mga namatayan, sa mga nalubog sa utang dahil sa kawalan ng maayos na protection. The least we can do is refuse to give them a political Merry Christmas in 2028.

Bahala na ang Diyos, they like to say.

But we were also given hands, voices, and votes.

Maybe it’s time we use them.

🟥 Etrikes and EVs in the Philippines: The Good, The Bad, the Ugly

[An MCT Commentary]

There's this e-trike driver I see every morning on my way to the market. 

He's probably in his 40s, weaving through the side streets with what looks like practiced ease. 

Sometimes I wonder if he knows that, as of late 2025, the government says it will start impounding his vehicle on January 2, 2026 if he crosses into the wrong road. 

Does he even know which roads are “wrong”?

This is the thing about the Philippines and electric vehicles. We want them. We need them. We passed a whole law about them. 

But somewhere between the legislation and the streets, things got messy.

And when I say messy, I mean the kind of chaos where you can’t tell if the government is trying to help or hurt the very thing it’s supposed to be promoting.

🟥 We Actually Need This

Shift to electric vehicles is not a lifestyle flex. It’s survival.

The transport sector takes up a big slice of the country’s greenhouse gas emissions. 

If nothing changes, emissions from transport could even triple by 2030. Triple. Think about that while you're stuck in EDSA traffic breathing in exhaust fumes.

More than half of our outdoor air pollution comes from vehicles. Our kids are breathing this in. Every single day.

And we’re bleeding money. Most of our oil is imported. Every time global oil prices spike, it shows up in the tricycle driver’s fuel budget, the jeepney driver’s daily kita, and the family grocery money.

So yes, we need electric vehicles. E-trikes, e-bikes, electric cars, electric jeepneys. All of it. Hindi na ito optional.

🟥 The Law We Passed

In April 2022, the government passed Republic Act 11697, the Electric Vehicle Industry Development Act or EVIDA. On paper, it looks strong.

The vision is big: by 2040, half of all vehicles in the country should be electric. To push that, tariffs on EVs and key components were dropped to zero for a set period, and the government lined up incentives for EV owners and operators.

Perks include things like priority registration, exemption from number coding, and faster processing for operators that go all-EV. 

There is also a national EV roadmap known as CREVI, which lays out short-term, medium-term, and long-term targets for infrastructure, manufacturing and skills. 

It is supposed to be updated regularly and integrated into the country’s energy and transport planning.

It reads like a country that knows where it wants to go.

Pero doon lang sa papel maganda.

🟥 When Good Intentions Meet Bad Execution

The e-trike program is the perfect example of how a good idea can be mishandled.

Back in the early 2010s, energy officials and lenders pushed a plan to roll out 100,000 e-trikes. The promise was to cut gasoline use and avoid a huge amount of carbon emissions every year. 

Then you see the numbers: one e-trike unit was priced at roughly the cost of a small car. Around the mid-four-hundred-thousand pesos per unit. A traditional tricycle costs a fraction of that.

No surprise: drivers didn’t line up. There were almost no takers.

So the government slashed the target from 100,000 units down to a few thousand. Project cost was trimmed as well. 

Even then, a lot of those e-trikes were reported to be sitting idle in warehouses or LGU lots because drivers and local officials either didn’t want them or found the payment terms unrealistic.

Big headline. Nice launch. Walang maayos na follow-through. 🤷‍♂️

🟥 The Infrastructure That Isn’t There

Then there’s the charging problem.

As of 2025, the Philippines has just a little over 900 publicly accessible charging stations. Some newer counts put it in the 900-plus to 900-plus-closer-to-1,000 range, with a mix of AC, DC, and battery-swapping units. Neighboring countries already have several thousand.

Most of our chargers are clustered in Metro Manila and nearby areas. Urban centers grab the bulk. Kung nasa probinsya ka, bahala ka sa buhay mo.

The target is around 7,300 charging stations by 2028. Right now, we’re far from that goal, and there isn’t a widely known, easy-to-use public map that ordinary people can rely on.

This sets up a loop that traps everyone.

Consumers hesitate to buy EVs because they’re scared of getting stranded with nowhere to charge. Investors hesitate to build charging stations because there aren’t enough EVs on the road yet. 

Meanwhile, agencies still find time to talk about bans and impounding e-trikes and e-bikes on national roads. Ang dali magbawal. Mas mahirap magtayo ng imprastraktura.

🟥 The Consumer Discipline Problem

Now, to be fair, hindi lang gobyerno ang may problema. Users too.

There are real safety issues tied to how e-trikes and e-bikes are used. Reports over the last few years show hundreds of incidents involving e-bikes, e-trikes, and e-scooters, with cases rising sharply since before the pandemic. 

In some places, the accident numbers have nearly tripled in a few years.

We see kids driving e-trikes in videos. Minors on public roads, sometimes with fellow minors as passengers, no adult in sight. 

Helmets feel optional to many. Small, low-speed vehicles end up in the middle of fast-moving lanes instead of staying on side streets or bike lanes.

A lot of units are unregistered. No plates, no insurance, no proof that the user understands basic road rules. May bahala-na attitude. 

The vehicles are cheap and convenient, so people jump in without thinking of the responsibility that comes with it.

Poor families are not the enemy here. The real danger comes from how these vehicles are used on the road, in ways that put drivers, passengers, pedestrians, and other motorists at risk.

🟥 Government Flip-Flopping at Its Finest

Then you have the policy roller coaster.

In mid-April 2024, MMDA announced and started implementing a ban on e-bikes and e-trikes on major roads in Metro Manila. Fines and possible impounding were part of the story.

A few days later, the President ordered MMDA to defer penalties for a month and focus first on warnings and information. So bawal, pero parang hindi pa bawal.

By October 2024, LTO suspended its earlier move requiring registration, licenses, and helmets for certain light electric vehicles, saying the rules needed review and might clash with EVIDA and other regulations. 

So for a time, users were told they did not need to register or get licenses.

Towards the end of November 2025, LTO announced that starting December 1, 2025, e-bikes and e-trikes would be banned from national roads and violators faced immediate impounding. Automatic daw ang impound.

Public backlash followed. Lawyers and advocates pointed out that under EVIDA, many light electric vehicles for private use are not even required to be registered with LTO. Lawmakers started questioning whether LTO had clear legal authority and promised to challenge the move in Congress.

By November 30, 2025, LTO stepped back again and pushed the start of impounding to January 2, 2026. 

December was rebranded as an “information drive” month, with personnel assigned to remind and “educate” users where they can and cannot bring their vehicles.

As of late 2025, LTO says strict enforcement of the ban and impounding on national roads will start on January 2, 2026, with no more extensions. But after all the flip-flops, people are right to ask if that deadline will hold or change again.

In the middle of all this, updated guidelines on which exact roads are off-limits are still being finalized and rolled out. Some users still have no clear idea if the street outside their house is counted as a “national road” or not.

For ordinary people, the message is chaos: register, don’t register, you need a license, you don’t, bawal dito, puwede diyan. The confusion is baked into the system.

🟥 The People Caught in the Middle

While all of this is happening, real people are just trying to earn enough to pay rent and buy ulam.

E-trike drivers can earn several hundred pesos in a day, sometimes near a thousand if the route and hours are good. After electricity and other expenses, they might bring home three to four hundred pesos. It’s not huge, but it’s what feeds their kids.

Comparisons between fuel and electricity costs show that for the same distance, a gasoline tricycle might spend a few hundred pesos on fuel, while an e-trike would spend a small fraction of that on electricity. 

The savings, even if it’s just a couple of hundred pesos a day, matter a lot for poor households.

A traditional tricycle driver pays for fuel and often a boundary fee, then takes home whatever’s left. 

An e-trike driver usually deals with electricity costs and possibly unit amortization, but with the right terms, the net income can be a little better and more stable.

For poor families, e-bikes and e-trikes also became a way to move. 

Parents can bring kids to school, take goods to the market, or reach health centers without paying fare every single time.

Now imagine you’re one of them. You bought an e-trike because government messaging said EVs are the future and the country is promoting them. 

You didn’t register because one set of rules told you it wasn’t required. Now another agency says they might impound your unit once you cross the wrong line on the road.

You’re not a lawyer. You’re just trying to earn a living.

🟥 The Contradictions We Can’t Ignore

This is the heart of the problem.

The state says it wants to promote electric vehicles for cleaner transport and energy security. It offers tax breaks, coding exemptions, and positive signals to investors and buyers.

At the same time, it:

- Bans e-bikes and e-trikes from main and national roads and threatens impoundment  

- Suspends registration and licensing, then later demands compliance with changing or unclear rules  

- Uses a traffic law from the 1960s in ways that clash with a 2022 EV law  

- Sets ambitious EV adoption targets but does not put up enough charging stations across the country  

- Talks about climate benefits while most of our electricity still comes from fossil fuels, which limits how “green” EVs really are unless the grid is cleaned up

You can’t push people toward EVs while making it confusing and risky to own one. 

You can’t call EVs a national priority and then treat low-income EV users as disposable.

There’s a gap between what the government says and what it actually does on the ground. And that gap is where public trust goes to die - all the time. 

🟥 What This Says About Us

This whole e-trike and EV story feels familiar because it mirrors how a lot of things work here.

We excel at writing laws, forming committees, doing presscons, and launching programs. We are not as good at coordination, long-term planning, clear communication, and consistent implementation.

Agencies act on their own. MMDA announces a ban. LTO issues its own set of rules based on an old law. DOTr and DOE point to EVIDA and long-term roadmaps. LGUs pass their own ordinances. Then Malacañang steps in to pause penalties for a bit when public backlash heats up.

In the middle of all that, e-trike drivers, delivery riders, and small families are left to decode the rules while worrying about the next meal.

What hurts is that this was supposed to be a hopeful story: cleaner air, lower daily operating costs, modernized transport, new jobs, and new industries. Instead, it risks turning into yet another case study in how inconsistent governance can kill good ideas.

🟥 What Should Happen (But Probably Won’t)

If we’re serious about making EVs work in the Philippines, we have to treat this as more than a PR line.

First, fix the legal conflict. EVIDA is newer and specific to EVs, while the old traffic law is broad and outdated. Key agencies need to reconcile their rules and end the contradictions. The country needs one clear, unified, and understandable set of rules for light EVs. Hindi puwedeng paiba-iba kada presscon.

Second, invest in infrastructure. Not just in Metro Manila, but in regional cities and growing towns. Give meaningful incentives for private firms to build public and semi-public charging stations. Require big developments to install chargers based on the EV law’s implementing rules.

Third, focus on education and proper licensing. Create a scaled license and registration system for light EVs, with fees and requirements that match their size and risk. Offer practical training and orientation sessions so users actually know road rules, right of way, and where their vehicles belong.

Fourth, design financing that works for the poor. There is no point in offering luxury-priced e-trikes to drivers who are barely surviving. Work with banks, co-ops, microfinance groups, and LGUs to create realistic payment plans for affordable units, paired with clear routes or green-transport programs.

Fifth, clean up the power mix. EVs only reach their full climate promise if the electricity that powers them is also becoming cleaner. Otherwise, we’re just shifting emissions from the exhaust pipe to the power plant.

And lastly, make policy with people, not just for people. Before announcing bans and deadlines, actually sit down with drivers, commuters, mobility advocates, local officials, and communities. Ask what they need to make EVs safe and workable in real life.

🟥 The Bottom Line

E-trikes and EVs in the Philippines could answer real needs. They can reduce our dependence on imported oil. They can help clean up the air. They can offer cheaper daily operating costs and a more stable livelihood for drivers, if unit prices and policies make sense.

Right now, though, the story is mixed.

The good: we have a law, a roadmap, and official recognition that EVs are part of the country’s future. There are incentives, pilot projects, and a slowly growing ecosystem of EV companies and charging providers.

The bad: infrastructure is lacking, information is confusing, and many policies don’t reflect how ordinary Filipinos actually live and move.

The ugly: agencies flip positions, rules contradict each other, and people who trusted the EV push now fear losing their vehicles or livelihoods because the state cannot settle on one clear direction.

The technology is not the villain here. The people using it are not the villains either. The real question is whether our institutions can keep up with the future they say they want.

For now, that e-trike driver on the way to the market is left guessing: saan ba talaga siya puwede, at hanggang kailan.

I find Vilma Santos’ recent statement defending her husband, Executive Secretary Ralph Recto, quite distasteful.

Not because she chose to stand by him. That is expected. She is his wife. But because of what she actually said, and how tone-deaf it sounds coming from someone who has spent years in public office.

I grew up watching Vilma Santos. Her movies were part of the background noise of my childhood, the kind you almost take for granted because they are always there. I memorized the rhythm of “I love you, Lucky” long before I understood what politics was, or what it meant for her to later become mayor, governor, and congresswoman.  

As an actress, she earned every bit of the title “Star for All Seasons.” As a politician, though, I have learned to separate my admiration for her film career from my disagreements with her public statements. This is one of those disagreements.

There is also a deeper clash here: how we see power and public service. I have always believed that public service should look like an inverted triangle. Her statement, on the other hand, sounds like the old upright one, where officials stand at the top and everyone else is beneath them.

🟥 What Vilma Actually Said

Ralph Recto, when he was Finance Secretary, approved the transfer of 60 billion pesos in PhilHealth reserve funds to the national treasury in 2024. Those funds were supposed to stay with PhilHealth under the Universal Health Care Act, to support benefits, strengthen the health system, and expand coverage.  

The Supreme Court later ruled, unanimously, that this transfer violated the law and ordered the government to return the 60 billion pesos to PhilHealth. The Court said the move breached the protections in the Universal Health Care Act that keep PhilHealth funds dedicated to social health insurance and health services.  

After that, complaints for plunder, graft, technical malversation, and grave misconduct were filed against Recto and former PhilHealth president Emmanuel Ledesma over the transfer. Recto has publicly said he will cooperate with the Ombudsman investigation and has described the transfer as a “common-sense” decision in line with the budget law at the time.

In the middle of all this, Vilma Santos spoke up.  

In a video that has now gone viral, she said: “Kaya niyo kami tinitira kasi nasa taas kami at kaya kayo tumitira kasi nasa baba kayo.”  

“You are attacking us because we are on top, and you are attacking because you are below.”  

Reports also say she suggested some critics might want to be in her husband’s position but cannot, which is why they attack.  

The message is clear enough:  

They are “up there.”  

We are “down here.”  

Criticism is framed as a reaction from people below, not as a normal part of holding public officials to account.

🟥 The Inverted Triangle of Service

In an inverted triangle, the people are at the broad top. They are the purpose, the priority, and the real center of power. Public officials are not the pinnacle. They are at the narrow bottom, carrying the weight, accountable upward to the citizens. It is very close to servant-leadership models: leaders exist to serve, not to be served, and their authority depends on consent and trust from those above them in that upside-down structure.

Now place that next to Vilma’s line: “kaya niyo kami tinitira kasi nasa taas kami at kaya kayo tumitira kasi nasa baba kayo.” In that single sentence, she draws a traditional hierarchy. She and her husband are “on top.” The rest of us are “below.” Criticism is not treated as a right of the people, but as a shot fired upward by those who do not share their status.

That goes against the inverted triangle of service. In that model, public officials do not speak as if they are perched above the public. They recognize that every question, every criticism, comes from the very people they are meant to serve. They do not see critics as frustrated outsiders clawing their way up, but as the true stakeholders reviewing how their employees are doing.

That is why her words felt so jarring to me. They did not just defend her husband. They also exposed how she seems to see the relationship between government officials and ordinary people.

🟥 Why Her Words Feel Wrong

There are many ways to defend a loved one. Vilma could have said she believes in her husband’s character. She could have said she trusts the legal process. She could have acknowledged the anger and worry of PhilHealth members and still stand by him.

Instead, she described critics as people attacking from below, taking shots at those “above” them.  

That framing sounds elitist because it assumes a higher moral or social position for those in power. It is dismissive because it shrinks serious concerns about a 60 billion peso health fund transfer into bitterness or jealousy.  

It also skips past important facts. The Supreme Court has already said the transfer violated the law and ordered the money returned to PhilHealth. Groups did not file plunder and graft complaints because they secretly want cabinet posts. They did it because they believe something serious and possibly criminal happened when funds legally reserved for health care were reclassified and moved.

When you are a public servant, you cannot treat criticism as a nuisance coming from “below.” You owe people an explanation. You owe them transparency. You owe them respect.

🟥 “Just Doing My Job” Is Not Enough

One line that keeps being used to soften this whole thing is that Recto was simply doing his job as Finance Secretary, following the appropriations law and acting in good faith. Some justices even pointed to good faith when they said he should not be held criminally liable, even though the transfer itself was struck down.

But the ruling still said the transfer was unconstitutional and must be undone. PhilHealth’s reserves, including so-called “excess” funds, are meant to expand benefits, lower contributions, and support universal health care, not to be treated as spare cash that can be shifted to the general fund.  

Good faith does not change the fact that 60 billion pesos meant for health care were taken out of PhilHealth’s hands. It does not repair the damage to trust in the health insurance system. It does not reassure members who worry that the money they pay in can be repurposed whenever it suits the government.

“Just doing my job” becomes dangerous when your job involves public money and constitutional limits. If that excuse is accepted without question, every official who signs off on a questionable transfer, unlawful order, or dubious reclassification can hide behind it.  

It becomes the easy script:  

I followed the law as I understood it.  

I relied on a circular.  

I was just doing my job.

If that script keeps getting rewarded, every corrupt or careless politician will eventually pick it up and use it.

🟥 The Triangle And PhilHealth

This is where the inverted triangle matters to me.  

If we see public service through that lens, a finance secretary is not a boss moving numbers around freely. He is a servant at the base of the triangle, responsible for protecting funds dedicated to health and for obeying both the Constitution and substantive laws like the Universal Health Care Act.  

Under that view, the 60 billion peso PhilHealth fund is not just another line in the budget. It is money that comes from people’s contributions, meant to help them survive a hospital stay, a surgery, a long illness. Any move that takes that money away from its legal purpose is not just a technical choice. It is a decision that can hurt real lives.

If the triangle is inverted, then:

- Officials expect hard questions when they make a choice like that.  
- The people are not “below.” They are the ones you answer to.  
- Saying “I was just doing my job” is not enough when the result clashes with the Constitution and with the clear intent of health law.

Describing critics as people from “below” who are merely hitting at those “above” shifts the focus to pride and status instead of law and responsibility. In an inverted triangle of service, that kind of thinking has no place. The people stand at the top of that structure, and officials are there to respond, not to remind everyone of their supposed rank.

🟥 Why It Hurts More Coming From Vilma

Hearing these words from Vilma hits differently for me.  

For many of us, she is not just another public figure. She is part of our memories. She has played mothers who give everything, women who stand up for justice, characters who fight from the edges of power.  

To then hear her, in real life, talk about being “on top” while everyone else is “below” cuts across the image many of us had of her. The line does not sound like the voice of the underdog or the voice of someone standing with ordinary people. It sounds like someone who sees herself on a higher step.

Her years as mayor, governor, and congresswoman are real. Many in Batangas still praise her work and presence. That history will not vanish because of one statement. But long experience in public office also means she should know how words like that land on people who are tired, overtaxed, and one sickness away from financial ruin.

🟥 Power, Service, And Place

In the end, this goes back to where we place ourselves in that triangle.  

If we keep the old, upright pyramid in our heads, then officials are at the top. The rest of us are at the bottom, trying to be heard. In that picture, criticism looks like disrespect, or envy, or people from “below” trying to hit those “above.”

If we flip the triangle, the order changes. The people stand at the top. Their needs, their rights, their health, and their money are the whole point of the structure. Officials, even the most powerful ones, are at the bottom, carrying that load and bound by the Constitution and basic decency to protect it.

That is why Vilma’s words, to me, are distasteful. They sound elitist and dismissive, yes. They also reveal a view of public office that does not fit with real service. If you believe in the inverted triangle, you do not talk as if you stand over the people. You understand that you stand under them, and that your work is to follow the Constitution, safeguard public funds, and face criticism without hiding behind status, fame, or family ties.

If you do not want to be questioned, public service is the wrong place to be.

172:
When a big name follows the page, I feel it in my chest first.

There’s that rush of kilig and excitement when I see a famous politician, an academic I look up to, a religious leader, or any “big name” hit that follow button. Part of me is grateful. Part of me feels seen, like this small corner of the internet somehow reached their world too.

But right beside that is a quiet dread.

Because I know myself as a blogger. When I need to talk about you, I don’t really hold back. I don’t disrespect you. I don’t do bardagulan. I’m not here to insult you for sport or drag you for engagement. But I will talk about you in vivid, sometimes uncomfortable detail, putting your words, choices, and patterns next to each other so people can see the bigger picture. This is the lane of opinion blogging, where facts, interpretation, and personal experience meet, and that’s the space I stand in.

🟥 Ego and first reactions

Here’s where ego comes in, and not just yours, mine too.

Most people’s first reaction to criticism is defensive. Even if the post is factual. Even if the tone is calm. Even if it’s not meant as a personal attack. The ego often hears, “You are the problem,” even when the message is, “This thing you did has consequences.” So the instinct is to push back, discredit, or get angry at the person giving the opinion.

So I’m realistic.

I know that no matter how fair or factual I try to be, some people will still feel disrespected. Some will decide I am out of line, biased, or walang modo. Some will attack my intelligence, my character, or my right to have an opinion at all. That’s how ego protects itself when it feels exposed.

But I stand by my opinion.

Not because I think I’m always right, but because I’m honest about where it comes from: it is what I see, what I understand, and what I believe, based on the information I have and the values I hold. This is commentary, not a court ruling. You are free to disagree. I am free to say, “This is how it looks from here.”

🟥 This is a personal blog

That’s why I keep repeating this: this is a personal blog.

I am not a journalist. I did not train in a newsroom, I don’t follow a newsroom’s code of objectivity, and I don’t claim to. What I do here is commentary. I read, I watch, I listen, and then I sit with my coffee and try to make sense of things in my own words.

I also don’t belong to any political party. I’m not a card-carrying member of any campaign. I don’t sit in strategy meetings. I don’t have any ambition to run for office or lobby for a post. I’m not building a political career here.

I’m a citizen with a page, sharing how I see things.

That’s it.

🟥 If you see a factual mistake

Now, about mistakes.

If you feel there is a factual mistake in a piece, email me.

Tell me what you saw, send a link, show me the numbers, point me to the source. It doesn’t automatically mean I will change my opinion. But I will research and confirm the facts first before I make changes. If I got a name, date, quote, or context wrong, I will correct it.

I’ve always listened to corrections. You can even ask Ms. Ellen Tordecillas. She has given me a nudge more than once when she sees a mistake, and I am grateful for that.

Opinion can stay.

Facts need to be right.

🟥 One rule in the comments

But I also have one clear rule: if you come to my comment section and disrespect me, it doesn’t matter who you are, I will block you.

You can disagree with me. You can tell me why you think I’m wrong. You can challenge my view. That is part of the deal when you post on the internet.

But if you attack me as a person, use insults, or treat my page like a dumping ground for rage, that’s a no.

I do not always agree with everything you write or say, whether you’re a politician, journalist, influencer, or anyone else. But I do not go to your comment section to attack you. I don’t camp under your posts to call you names or question your right to exist online.

I expect the same basic respect in return.

Disagree with the idea, argue with the take, but don’t dehumanize the person behind it.

Because like I said from the start, I will talk about you in vivid detail.

I will lay out what you said, what you did, what it connects to. I will ask hard questions. I will probably disappoint you at some point. But even when the critique is sharp, it comes from someone who cares enough to pay attention, to listen, to cross-check, and to take the time to write, instead of just staying quiet and scrolling past.

In a strange way, it will always be part critique, part boundary-setting, and part love letter to the person who clicked follow and stepped into this messy conversation with the rest of us.

🟥 When the Propaganda Machine Starts Telling the Truth  

[An MCT opinion piece]

There’s something deeply unsettling to me about the latest Publicus Asia survey. 

Not because the numbers are bad—I’ve seen bad numbers before. 

Not because it shows Marcos at historic lows—approval in the low twenties, trust in the mid-teens, and a big chunk of Filipinos saying they’d support his resignation. I expected that.  

What unsettles me is who’s telling me this.  

Publicus Asia isn’t a neutral little polling shop in the corner. 

It’s the firm founded by Malou Tiquia, a veteran operator whose career was built on lobbying and campaign management for politicians and parties. 

From the start, I’ve seen Publicus less as a detached research house and more as a campaign machine that also does surveys. 

When House Deputy Speaker Lito Atienza called their work “biased” and said their surveys smelled like political propaganda, he was basically putting into words what many of us had already felt about the outfit. 

Online, too, people have long dismissed them as a PR firm pretending to be a pollster, with some tagging Tiquia as closely aligned with Duterte and his camp.  

So when a firm with that kind of reputation suddenly releases survey results that show the sitting president in freefall, I can’t just clap and say, “Finally, honesty.” 

Something changed. The question in my mind is: what exactly happened?  

🟥 The Senators That Don’t Quite Add Up  

What first grabbed my attention was not even Marcos’ numbers, but the ranking of senators. In the Pahayag 2025 End-of-Year survey, the “most favorable” senators list looks like this:  

- Bam Aquino on top, in the 50s in net favorable.  
- Kiko Pangilinan close behind.  
- Risa Hontiveros right there with them.  
- Then Bong Go and Rodante Marcoleta rounding out the top five.  

That means three opposition or reform-leaning figures—Bam, Kiko, Risa—clustered at the top, with two Duterte-aligned names—Go and Marcoleta—still in the inner circle. 

Meanwhile, a figure like Jinggoy Estrada sinks to the bottom, tagged as one of the least favorable among the sitting senators.  

On its own, this result actually makes sense to me. 

Bam and Kiko pulled off comeback wins in the 2025 midterms. They campaigned on clean governance and reforms, and many voters, especially younger ones, clearly bought into that. 

Risa has built a reputation as a consistent human rights and good governance advocate, often seen as the face of the institutional opposition in the Senate. 

Bong Go and Marcoleta, on the other hand, still enjoy the loyalty of Duterte supporters and certain media ecosystems.  

But when I line this Publicus result up against more conventional, face-to-face polling, the picture becomes messy. 

Pulse Asia’s 2025 data still show Bong Go, Raffy Tulfo, and Alan Peter Cayetano leading Senate performance ratings with very high approval scores. 

Commentaries on those numbers talk about how Go and Tulfo continue to ride on their media presence and populist branding. 

Other polls also don’t consistently place Bam, Kiko, and Risa all in the top cluster at the same time.  

So I end up with a senatorial picture that feels half-familiar, half-off. 

Familiar, because it reflects real gains by reform-minded and opposition figures. 

Off, because it doesn’t match the usual dominance of personalities like Go and Tulfo that we see in more traditional, face-to-face samples. 

This is what I mean when I say the results look realistic but out of character. They are believable, but they don’t fully line up with how Philippine polling usually looks.  

🟥 The Numbers Feel Right, But the Messenger Makes Me Pause  

When I look at the rest of the recent numbers, they also don’t feel fake to me. The trend matches what other pollsters have been reporting: Marcos’ satisfaction and approval ratings sliding downward through 2025, hitting their lowest levels since he took office. 

Different surveys have been telling the same story from different angles—falling trust, rising dissatisfaction, and growing disillusionment with how he’s handling scandals and governance.  

Publicus’ own deck reinforces that picture. 

They show almost half of respondents viewing the country as weak, with only about a quarter saying things are strong. They register disapproval of the Marcos administration breaching the 50 percent mark, which is a symbolic line for any sitting president. 

To me, those figures don’t look like shock-value outliers; they look like confirmation of a pattern that has been forming all year.  

What really catches my attention is how explicitly the survey frames public anger around corruption. 

Filipinos saying they’re tired of corruption isn’t new—but when the same instrument ties that frustration to top officials, flood control projects, and fears that tax money is being stolen, I feel like I’m reading an indictment disguised as a survey. 

When questions about drug-use allegations produce answers that large numbers would lose trust completely if those claims are proven, I see a presidency sitting on political quicksand.  

The numbers, in other words, feel believable. The problem for me is not the data alone—it’s the marriage between this data and this particular pollster.  

🟥 My Problem With Publicus Didn’t Vanish Overnight  

I don’t suddenly forget the baggage that comes with Publicus just because they’re now releasing results that line up with my own criticism of the administration. 

This is still the same company that openly brands itself as a one-stop campaign shop, not just a research institute. 

It’s the same outfit that relies on an online panel whose representativeness has been questioned by statisticians and ordinary netizens alike. 

That critique doesn’t disappear just because their latest output is politically convenient for me.  

For years, I watched Publicus numbers being waved around online to boost certain candidates and interests.

I also watched people push back, pointing out that when your business model is campaigns plus polling, there will always be doubts about how those two sides influence each other. 

I’ve seen analyses noting that some figures in their orbit had ties or work that intersected with Marcos-aligned politics, which only deepened my suspicion.  

So when I now see them publishing a deck that basically says, “The emperor has no clothes,” I don’t automatically shout “finally credible!” Instead, I hear a quieter, more cautious question in my head: are they telling the truth because they’ve changed, or because they have no choice?  

🟥 Watching Malou Tiquia Read the Room  

What really made this turn feel real to me was not just the numbers, but Malou Tiquia’s own words. 

When I watched her talk about the flood control scandal, I could tell something had hardened. 

She pointed to “the leader and the cousin” as being at the top of that mess—clearly referring to Marcos and Romualdez.  

She reminded everyone that nothing big moves without the president’s blessing, and that the budget doesn’t become law without his signature. 

She recalled how Marcos claimed to have read the entire budget and found nothing suspicious, only to later turn around and act like he was exposing other people’s wrongdoing. 

To me, that sounded less like analysis and more like someone calling out a performance she had seen too many times.  

Then she went after the economic side: lack of capital formation, weak markets, stolen funds, and no real accountability months after the big “mahiya kayo” speech. 

She emphasized that the only person facing cases was the whistleblower, not the big players. 

Hearing that from someone like her—someone who has worked in, around, and for power for decades—told me this wasn’t just rhetorical positioning. It felt like a deliberate break.  

Long before this survey, I already had a running joke on my page: I’ve posted more than once that one of the most dangerous kinds of DDS is a DDS with a PhD or a title. 

In my head, Malou Tiquia is exactly that type—highly educated, highly connected, and fully capable of using the language of analysis to defend or attack, depending on where her interests and alliances lie.  

And this is where my own conflict with Malou Tiquia comes in. 

I agree with her criticism of Marcos and Romualdez, but I also can’t ignore that her public record shows consistent positions and commentary that align with Duterte and his camp. 

For me, her attacks on Marcos sound less like a clean break from rotten politics and more like an echo of the Duterte camp’s war against him, with all the self-serving interests that come with that. 

My view of the Dutertes is already very low: they are a danger to the Philippines, low-quality politicians with no real leadership or management ability, and with no moral authority to lead anything, much less a country. 

So when someone closely associated with them suddenly presents herself as a truth-teller on corruption, I don’t just listen to what she’s saying; I also ask who stands to benefit if Marcos falls.  

As someone who writes about politics, I’ve learned to listen carefully when old insiders begin to talk like that. They don’t choose their words casually. They know how dangerous and how useful those words can be.  

🟥 Where This Leaves Me: Suspicious, But Not Surprised  

So here’s where I end up as a blogger and as a citizen: I accept that the Publicus survey is realistic, but I stay suspicious about the institution and the timing. 

The numbers line up with broader trends—Marcos’ decline, public exhaustion with corruption, and growing willingness to imagine a future without him. 

On that level, the survey confirms what many of us already feel in our gut.  

But I also see a different story behind the charts: the story of how people and institutions that once helped protect a president are now quietly stepping away. 

When even a pollster with a history of being seen as close to power starts publishing data that damages that power, I don’t celebrate; I take note. Because it usually means the elites are already thinking about a transition.  

That’s why, instead of feeling relieved by this unusual honesty, I feel more cautious. 

I know that when the propaganda machine starts telling the truth, it’s rarely because it has grown a conscience. 

Most of the time, it simply means the lie has become too expensive, and the people who built the machine are now trying to survive the next chapter.  

As someone who writes Morning Coffee Thoughts as an advocacy, that’s the part that keeps me up: not whether Marcos’ numbers are really that low—they probably are—but what kind of political animal replaces him when the same machinery just redirects its loyalty to a new client.

🟥 Disclaimer: 

I am aware that Ms. Malou Tiquia follows Morning Coffee Thoughts. Her follow is noted and I am grateful, but it does not change my views on their record, their alliances, or their role in our politics. This piece reflects my own reading of the facts and of power.

🟥 The Guarantee

[A work of fiction  by MCT]

Manila, December 24, 2025, 11:47 PM

Maria's water broke on the jeepney.

The liquid pooled beneath her on the cracked vinyl seat, mixing with the grime of a thousand passengers. The driver didn't stop. He glanced at the rearview mirror, eyes meeting her husband José's panicked face, then looked away. Three more stops until they reach the hospital. The fluorescent lights overhead flickered in rhythm with the potholes.

José pressed his palm against the small of her back. His callused fingers—still smelling of diesel from his twelve-hour shift at the port—trembled. In his other hand, crumpled papers: their PhilHealth Member Data Record, printed at an internet café for five pesos per page. The ink had smeared in the rain.

The hospital's emergency room buzzed with the mechanical hum of old air conditioning units that cooled nothing. Plastic chairs, filled. Bodies slumped against walls. A child coughed wetly in the corner. The smell of Lysol barely masked something older, more organic.

"PhilHealth number?" The admitting clerk didn't look up. Her fingers hovered over a keyboard missing three keys.

José slid the papers across the counter. Behind him, Maria doubled over, gripping the metal railing bolted to the wall. Her knuckles went white.

The clerk's eyes scanned the document. Then stopped.

"Suspended po. Hindi updated ang contributions." Her voice carried no emotion, just fact. Contributions not updated. Three months behind. José's employer had pocketed the deductions.

"Pero—" José's voice cracked. "But we need—"

"You need a guarantee letter. From your senator or congressman. Try mo din sa barangay captain. Kung wala, a promissory note from a relative with valid ID and proof of income." She was already looking past him, toward the next person in line.

Outside, the city glittered. Parols—Christmas lanterns—hung from electrical wires, their colors bleeding into the smog. Somewhere, a karaoke machine belted an off-key drunk version of "Silent Night."

José ran. His tsinelas slapped against wet pavement. The barangay hall was dark, locked. The captain's house—a three-story concrete structure with a new SUV parked outside—had its lights on. He rang the doorbell. Once. Twice. A dog barked. A security camera's red light blinked.

No answer.

He tried calling his brother. The phone rang and rang. No answer. He called his sister. "Ate, please, kailangan ko ng guarantee lett...—" The line went dead. He ran out of load. 

When he returned, empty-handed, Maria was on a bench outside the ER. A nurse—young, maybe twenty-five—knelt beside her, timing contractions with her phone.

"Seven minutes apart," the nurse whispered. Her name tag read ANGELA. "I can't admit her without the documents. But I can stay here."

Angela led them to a janitor’s storage room on the second floor, a cramped space that smelled of bleach and damp concrete. Worn mops and brooms—walis tambo and walis tingting—were propped in one corner beside a stained yellow mop pail, while plastic bottles of cleaning liquids crowded a rusty metal shelf. She spread her jacket on a dry patch of floor between the buckets and supply boxes, then covered it with unused hospital gowns, still sealed in their thin plastic.

"I'm sorry," she said. "This is all—"

Maria shook her head, already lowering herself down. "Okay lang."

The contractions came harder. Maria's breath turned to moans, then screams she tried to swallow. José held her hand. His own hands shook so violently he thought he might break her fingers.

Angela worked with what she had: a flashlight, because the room's bulb was burned out. Gloves from her pocket. A sterile birthing kit she'd taken from the delivery room, an act that could cost her her job.

At 12:41 AM, December 25th, the baby came.

His cry cut through the silence—sharp, clear, furious at the world he'd entered.

Angela wrapped him in hospital blankets stamped PROPERTY OF DOH. She placed him on Maria's chest. The mother's tears fell onto the newborn's face, and he quieted.

Through the window, the city continued. Somewhere, a church bell rang. Families gathered around tables heavy with ham and queso de bola. Children tore open gifts wrapped in glossy paper.

In the storage room, José stared at his son. The baby's fingers, impossibly small, gripped his thumb.

"Salamat sa Diyos. Salamat sa iyo, Ma'am Angela." Maria whispered.

Angela checked Maria's bleeding, monitored the baby's breathing. When her shift ended at 2 AM, she didn't leave. She sat against the wall, the glow of her phone illuminating her face as she typed an incident report she would never submit.

Outside the door, the hospital moved on. Gurneys squeaked. Machines beeped. Someone screamed for a doctor.

Inside, three people and a newborn existed in a pocket of stillness.

José finally spoke. "Salamat. Thank you."

Angela looked at the baby, then at the crumpled PhilHealth papers on the floor, now stained with amniotic fluid and blood.

"He's beautiful," she said.

And he was. Despite everything. Despite the guarantee letters that never came, the contributions stolen by an employer whose name appeared on no documents, the system that had no room for them.

The baby slept, his chest rising and falling, his tiny hand still gripping his father's thumb.

Outside, in the waiting area, someone had left a small parol on the nurses' station. Its light—powered by a single AA battery—cast star-shaped shadows on the walls.

By morning, Angela would falsify the admission papers, claiming the birth happened after a Good Samaritan paid the deposit. The hospital administrator would never investigate—too much paperwork, and it was Christmas. Maria and the baby would be moved to the charity ward. José would return to the port, working double shifts to pay a debt that didn't legally exist.

But in that room, in those hours between midnight and dawn, none of that had happened yet.

There was only this: a family. A kindhearted nurse. A child.

Light, in a place that had none.
