🟥 The Promil Kid's Hostage Crisis: How Leandro Leviste Is Destroying the DPWH Scandal for a Media Stunt

Part 2: Death by a Thousand Teasers 

[MCT | Opinion] 

[Paskong-pasko pero gigil na gigil ako. So, pa-isa lang po.]

If you thought getting the documents was the problem, wait until you see what Leandro Leviste is doing with them.

For over three months—from September 4 to December 23—the Batangas congressman has played a game that would make reality TV producers jealous: drip, tease, hint, defer, wait for permission, check with Daddy Dizon, consult the authorities, think about the consequences, worry about the implications. 

All while the most important evidence in the biggest corruption scandal of this administration slowly loses whatever legal value it might have had.

Instead of serving the public interest with immediate disclosure, he has turned the evidence into a bargaining chip, constantly altering the rules of engagement to keep the spotlight on himself.

🟥 The Timeline of a Trainwreck

The sequence of events reveals the true nature of this delay:

September 4, 2025: Leviste claims he received the documents from Undersecretary Catalina Cabral with Secretary Vince Dizon's blessing. Security logs tell a different story—they show him leaving with pizza, not papers. But let's be generous and assume he got something.

September to November: Radio silence. The congressman sits on documents that allegedly detail a multi-billion peso corruption scheme involving senators, congressmen, undersecretaries, and private individuals. 

Documents that could have helped ongoing investigations by the Independent Commission for Infrastructure (ICI) and the Office of the Ombudsman.

November 11 and 19: He shows the documents to the ICI. November 26: He shares them with the Ombudsman. Good, right? Not quite—because he doesn't release them publicly. He doesn't even release them to other investigators. He controls who sees what, when, and how much.

December 19: Cabral dies under suspicious circumstances in Benguet.

December 21: Suddenly, Leviste remembers he has these documents! In a Facebook post, he announces their existence—but says he needs to wait for DPWH Secretary Dizon's approval before releasing them publicly. The documents he's had for three and a half months.

December 23: After mounting pressure and after the DPWH already turned over Cabral's computers to the Ombudsman for forensic examination, Leviste finally releases... a 19-page summary. Not the complete files. Not the full evidence. A curated highlight reel, posted on his Facebook page.

He is conducting a publicity tour disguised as whistleblowing.

🟥 The Legal Catastrophe

Here's what Leviste either doesn't understand or doesn't care about: evidence has to be handled properly or it becomes worthless in court.

In Philippine law, the chain of custody is sacred. For evidence to be admissible, you need to prove: (1) who had it, (2) when they had it, (3) where it was stored, (4) how it was transferred, and (5) that it wasn't tampered with at any point. This is basic Criminal Procedure 101. This is why police mark evidence at crime scenes, document every transfer, store items in sealed containers, and maintain meticulous logs.

What did Leviste do? He allegedly took documents from a government official's office (with or without permission—the security logs suggest "without"), kept them in his personal possession for three months, showed them selectively to some authorities but not others, made public statements about their contents without releasing them, and then posted a partial version on Facebook after the original source died under suspicious circumstances.

Every single step of that process is a chain-of-custody nightmare.

Let's say, hypothetically, that these documents eventually lead to criminal charges. Any halfway competent defense lawyer will have a field day:

- "Your Honor, how do we know these are the original documents?"
- "How do we know they weren't altered during the three months they were in Congressman Leviste's possession?"
- "Why are there discrepancies between what the Ombudsman received and what was posted on Facebook?"
- "If these documents were so important, why did the congressman sit on them for months while investigations were ongoing?"
- "Why did he only release them after the original source—the only person who could authenticate them—died?"

The prosecution's response? "Trust us, these are real."

Good luck with that in court.

The Liberal Party already questioned the authenticity of Leviste's documents. 

Security logs contradicted his version of how he obtained them. And now, after months of personal custody and selective disclosure, these documents have been so compromised that they're practically exhibit A in "How to Destroy Evidence While Pretending to Be a Reformer."

🟥 The Political Spectacle

But here's the thing: unlike some senators and congressmen, Leviste isn't stupid. He went to good schools. He built a billion-peso company. He sold his solar company to Meralco for P34 billion, making him the youngest billionaire in the Philippines. He won a congressional seat with 268,764 votes—the most votes in any congressional race in the history of Batangas. He knows what he's doing.

So why the piecemeal release? Why the dramatic "I'm waiting for permission" act when he could have simply turned everything over to the proper authorities on September 5 and let the institutions do their jobs?

Because this was about leverage and never about accountability. 

WTF, right? 

Think about what Leviste accomplished with his three-month hostage crisis:

1. He stayed in the headlines. Every few weeks, there's a new Leviste story. "Leviste has documents." "Leviste won't release documents." "Leviste shows documents to ICI." "Leviste waiting for DPWH approval." "Leviste finally releases documents." He's been the main character in this story since September, not because of what he's done, but because of what he might do.

2. He protected his allies. Notice how he's very selective about who he goes after? He called out Bicol Saro party-list Representative Brian Raymund Yamsuan. He named former officials. But when it came to his own mother, Senator Loren Legarda, who allegedly had around P1 billion in insertions? Oh, that's okay, she didn't know about those projects, they were the smallest amount, nothing to worry about.

3. He positioned himself as the hero. By controlling the release, he's not just a witness or a whistleblower—he's the gatekeeper. He decides who gets exposed, when, and how much. He is exercising raw power rather than practicing transparency.

4. He is trivializing the entire scandal. Here's the worst part: by turning this into a soap opera about when and whether he'll release documents, Leviste is moving the conversation from "massive government corruption" to "will the congressman release the list?" The DPWH scandal—with its ghost projects, fake receipts, substandard flood control systems, kickbacks, and dead whistleblowers—has become background noise to the Leandro Leviste Show.

🟥 When Incompetence Destroys What It Claims to Defend

Remember the Pepedederalismo scandal?

In August 2018, then-Presidential Communications Assistant Secretary Mocha Uson and blogger Drew Olivar released a video supposedly promoting Duterte's federalism campaign. Instead of explaining the constitutional reform, they created a vulgar, sexually suggestive skit that reduced "federalism" to "pepe" (vagina) and "dede" (breasts). The video went viral for all the wrong reasons.

Senate President Vicente "Tito" Sotto called it "beyond comical." Critics across the political spectrum condemned it as offensive and juvenile. By mid-2019, a Cabinet member admitted the video had "poured very cold water on the federalism initiative." The push for federalism—already struggling—never recovered from the embarrassment.

In Mocha and Drew's case, their crass incompetence accidentally did the country a favor. Duterte's version of federalism was widely criticized as a power grab. Their idiocy helped kill a bad idea.

Leandro Leviste is doing the exact same thing—except in reverse.

Where Mocha Uson used a three-minute dance video to destroy federalism, Leviste is using a three-month document hostage crisis to destroy the DPWH corruption investigation. Where Uson's vulgarity made federalism look ridiculous, Leviste's piecemeal releases and Facebook theatrics are making the DPWH scandal look like a political game instead of a criminal matter.

The methods are different. The damage is the same.

Both took serious issues—constitutional reform, government corruption—and turned them into social media spectacles that undermined public confidence and legal credibility. Both prioritized getting attention over getting results. Both thought they were helping when they were actually sabotaging.

The cruel irony? Mocha Uson was a former sexy dancer with no political experience who stumbled into government through Duterte's patronage. She was unqualified, out of her depth, and everyone knew it. When her incompetence damaged the federalism campaign, nobody was surprised.

Leandro Leviste is a billionaire entrepreneur who sold his company for P34 billion. He went to good schools. He won a congressional seat with the highest vote count in Batangas history. He has access to lawyers, PR teams, and political strategists.

And yet here he is, making the same fundamental error as Mocha Uson: mistaking publicity for advocacy, confusing social media engagement with actual reform, and destroying the very cause he claims to champion through sheer tactical incompetence.

At least Mocha eventually had the decency to resign when the backlash became unbearable. She understood, at some level, that she had damaged something important.

Leviste? He's still going, still posting, still teasing, still positioning himself as the hero while the legal value of his evidence disintegrates with every passing day.

If the DPWH investigation collapses because defense lawyers successfully argue that the evidence was compromised by months of personal custody and selective social media releases, Leviste will have accomplished something truly remarkable: he will have proven that you don't need to be as crude as Mocha Uson to torpedo an important national initiative.

You just need to be just as incompetent—and twice as arrogant about it.

🟥 The Missing Context Nobody Wants to Talk About

Let's address the elephant in the room: security logs from September 4 show Leviste leaving Cabral's office without any visible documents—just pizza. The next day, September 5, Cabral appeared at a Senate Blue Ribbon Committee hearing with a bandaged finger, which witnesses say was injured in a struggle over documents.

Leviste's version: Cabral willingly handed him the documents with Dizon's approval, everyone was professional and transparent.

The evidence: Doesn't quite match that story.

Now, here's the question nobody in mainstream media seems willing to ask: If Leviste obtained these documents improperly—maybe even forcibly—would he ever turn them over completely to authorities who might verify that?

Or would he instead dole them out piecemeal, always maintaining control, always claiming he's waiting for permission, always positioning himself as the noble reformer who just wants to do things properly?

You tell me which scenario fits the facts better.

🟥 The Promil Kid's Master Plan

This is the part where we need to remember who Leandro Leviste is.

He's not a civil society activist. He's not a journalist. He's not some idealistic reformer who stumbled into politics. He's the son of former Batangas Governor Antonio Leviste and Senator Loren Legarda. He was literally featured in Promil milk commercials as a "gifted child." He sold his solar company to Meralco for P34 billion, making him the youngest billionaire in the Philippines. Then he ran for Congress and won by a landslide.

This is a man who's been groomed for power his entire life.

And now he's using the DPWH scandal—which involves real corruption, real ghost projects, real dead bodies—as a stepping stone for whatever political ambitions he has. As I detailed in Part 1, he's clearly positioning himself for 2028, whether for Senate or something higher.

The piecemeal release serves that goal perfectly. Maximum exposure, minimum accountability. He gets to be the crusader without actually crushing anyone who might be useful later. He gets to attack the system while protecting the people he needs to protect (like his mother). He gets headlines without the legal complications that would come from proper whistleblowing.

It's brilliant, really. Morally bankrupt, but brilliant.

🟥 What Should Have Happened

Let me spell out what proper procedure looks like, just so we're clear:

September 4: Leviste receives documents. He immediately takes them, unaltered and in full, to the Office of the Ombudsman.

September 5: He files a formal complaint with supporting documentation. The Ombudsman takes custody of the evidence, documents the chain of custody, and begins forensic verification.

September 6: He issues a public statement: "I have turned over evidence to the proper authorities and will cooperate fully with their investigation."

September 7 onwards: He keeps his mouth shut and lets the investigators do their jobs. No teasers. No Facebook posts. No selective leaks. No waiting for permission from the very department that's being investigated.

Proper evidence handling serves the public interest and secures convictions.

But Leviste took a different path. Instead, he turned crucial evidence into a social media campaign, and in the process, he may have ensured that the people responsible for the DPWH scandal never face justice.

🟥 The Damage Is Done

Here's the tragic reality: even if every single name on those lists is guilty, even if every allegation is true, even if billions of pesos really were stolen through ghost projects and kickbacks—the way Leviste "is handling" this evidence "is compromising" any future prosecution.

Defense lawyers are already salivating. "Your Honor, these documents have been in the personal custody of a partisan political actor for months, were selectively released to the media before proper forensic analysis, and only surfaced publicly after the original source died. We move to exclude them as inadmissible."

And you know what? They might win that motion.

The real criminals—the ones who actually stole from flood control projects, who built substandard infrastructure that put lives at risk, who took kickbacks while people drowned—those people are probably sleeping just fine tonight. Because thanks to the Promil Kid's publicity stunt, the evidence that might have convicted them is now legally toxic.

Meanwhile, Undersecretary Maria Catalina Cabral is dead. Former officials have resigned. Reputations have been destroyed. And Leandro Leviste is positioning himself as the hero who exposed it all.

In reality, he is trivializing the scandal rather than exposing it. He is turning a corruption scandal into a reality show. And in doing so, he may have killed any chance of real accountability.

🟥 Why am I so pissed of even if it's Christmas Day?

There's a special kind of cynicism required to take evidence of massive corruption and use it primarily for personal political gain. To see a government scandal that cost lives and livelihoods and think, "How can I leverage this for my career?"

That's what we're watching with Leandro Leviste.

He wants credit for being a reformer without doing the hard, messy work of actual reform. He wants to attack corruption while protecting his allies. He wants to be the hero of the story without ensuring the villains actually face justice.

And worst of all, he's convinced a lot of people that his political spectacle is the same thing as accountability.

It is a dangerous illusion.

Real accountability would mean proper evidence handling. Real accountability would mean letting institutions do their jobs. Real accountability would mean accepting that you might not get the media spotlight if you do things properly.

But that would require putting country before personal ambition. And given everything we've seen from the Promil Kid, that seems like too much to ask.

So here we are: three months of teasers later, with compromised evidence, questionable authenticity, a broken chain of custody, and a congressman who thinks Facebook is an appropriate venue for releasing evidence in what should be criminal proceedings.

The DPWH scandal deserved better. The Filipino people deserved better. And the victims of substandard flood control projects—some of whom literally died because of this corruption—definitely deserved better.

What they got instead was the Leandro Leviste Show, Season 1.

I can't wait to see what he does for Season 2.

I pressed my palm against the cold.  

White flakes sat there for a second. Then they melted into water, tiny rivers running between my fingers.  

Ganito pala 'to.  

This is what snow feels like.  

Fifty-two years old and I'm standing here with my hand stretched out like a five-year-old. I couldn't stop smiling. I probably looked ridiculous to everyone else, but I didn't care. I kept catching snowflakes. Watching them disappear. Catching more.  

You grow up in the Philippines and you see snow in movies. In Christmas cards. On TV specials where white people build snowmen and have snowball fights. 

It's pretty. It's foreign. It's not yours.  

You accept that some things are just not part of your story.  

🟥 The Things We Tell Ourselves We'll Never Have

There's a version of yourself you create early on. The tropical version. 

The one who sweats through traffic. Who knows the exact feeling of monsoon rain hitting hot pavement. Who understands humidity as a constant companion.

I grew up poor. I was a street kid in the 80s. I never even dreamed of visiting the US or Canada. Hindi siya option. Hindi siya kasama sa imagination. The goal back then was simpler: makasurvive, makatapos kahit paano, makaahon kahit konti.

Snow doesn't fit into that version.

So you don't think about it much. You don't add it to your list of dreams because it feels too far away. Too expensive. Too impractical. Too late.

🟥 Banff: A Gentle First Touch

Before that quiet moment with my hand open to the sky, we were in Banff, the place I had been whispering to God about for years.

My wish finally came true.

At Banff, the temperature was friendlier, almost kind. I even managed to walk around without wearing my gloves for a while. 

The snow on the ground was fresh, soft under my boots, like the world had just been washed. People were everywhere, bundled up, taking photos, laughing, living their normal winter lives while I tried to absorb every detail.

For them, it was just another cold day.

For the former street kid in the 80s who never imagined leaving the Philippines, it felt like stepping into someone else’s dream and realizing, quietly, that it had somehow become your own.

For me, it was history.

🟥 Lake Louise: When The Sky Joined In

But it was at Lake Louise that my heart really jumped.  

As soon as we arrived, it was snowing.  

Not just snow on the ground, but snow falling from the sky. My first time to see it actually falling. The kind of scene I’d only seen in movies and tamang inggit Facebook posts.  

I had to catch some.  

I stretched my hand out and watched the flakes land on my glove. Then I found myself struggling to see the individual snowflake, leaning closer, trying not to breathe too hard so it wouldn’t melt too fast.  

And it’s true what they say: they really are different from each other, like fingerprints. Tiny, delicate patterns, here for a moment, then gone. You don’t get to own them. You just get to witness them.  

Standing there, with that lake tucked under ice and white, mountains wrapped in snow, and the sky still sending down more, I felt both very small and very seen.  

🟥 The Weight of Firsts

Experiencing winter for the first time at fifty-two carries a different feeling than it would at twenty-two or thirty-two.  

There's no arrogance in it.  

No sense of entitlement. No assumption that the world owes you these moments.  

Instead, there's gratitude. Sharp and clear and almost painful. Because you know how easily this could have never happened. How many circumstances had to align. How much of life is just luck and timing and being in the right place when the door opens.  

Ang dami kong iniisip.  

I thought about everyone back home who'll never see this. Who'll never feel snow melt on their skin. Not because they don't want to, but because the world doesn't give everyone the same access to wonder.  

That's not fair. But it's true.  

And standing there in the cold, I felt the weight of that truth. The privilege of being here. The randomness of getting to experience something millions of people only see in pictures.  

🟥 Feeling Like a Kid

But here's what I didn't expect: the pure, uncomplicated joy.  

I made a snowball. My first one ever.  

It was smaller than I thought it would be. Harder to pack together. My hands were freezing and clumsy. But I held it up like a trophy. I laughed out loud.  

Fifty-two years old and I'm laughing alone in the snow.  

That's the thing about wonder. It doesn't care how old you are. It doesn't ask for permission or wait for the right moment. It just shows up and reminds you that the world can still surprise you.  

I forgot about my back pain. My tired knees. The way my body reminds me daily that I'm not young anymore.  

For a few minutes, I was just a person experiencing something new.  

That's enough.  

🟥 What This Means

I don't know if this story has a bigger meaning. I'm not trying to make it more than it is.  

But I do know this: we tell ourselves stories about what's possible and what's not. About what kind of life we're supposed to live. About what we deserve and what we've missed.  

Sometimes those stories are wrong.  

Sometimes you get a second chance at firsts. Sometimes the world opens up in ways you stopped expecting. Sometimes you're fifty-two and touching snow for the first time and feeling every bit of wonder you thought you'd lost.  

I stayed outside until my fingers went completely numb.  

Then I went inside, made coffee, and looked out the window at the white covering everything.  

Ganito pala talaga.  

Right now, as I write this, it's two in the morning. Outside, it's minus sixteen degrees, and according to the weather reports we need to brace for more snow in the next hours. But you know what? When that starts, I’ll be outside, making memories, catching whatever snow I can, because I might not be able to experience this again.  

To my sisters-in-law and their husbands: thank you for the warm, beautiful welcome, for the jackets, the rides, the hot meals, the laughter, and for making this first winter feel less like a vacation and more like being folded gently into family.  

And to my wife and daughter: thank you for making Christmas 2025 special for me in a way I will never forget. 

For the love, the patience, the sacrifices seen and unseen, and for cheering me on like this fifty-two-year-old “first timer” was doing something brave, not just something late. 

You are my home, wherever in the world I stand in the snow.
177:
🟥 When “Good Faith” Becomes a Get-Out-of-Jail-Free Card

The plunder complaint filed against Executive Secretary Ralph Recto and former PhilHealth president Emmanuel Ledesma Jr. should force us to confront a simple question: at what point does “just following the law” stop being an excuse for violating it?

On December 22, 2025, the Save the Philippines Coalition brought a 15-page complaint to the Office of the Ombudsman, accusing Recto and Ledesma of technical malversation, plunder, and grave misconduct over the transfer of 60 billion pesos in PhilHealth reserve funds to the national treasury. They filed this just weeks after the Supreme Court unanimously struck down that same transfer as unconstitutional and ordered the amount returned to PhilHealth.

What makes this episode infuriating is not only the size of the amount involved. It is the ease with which public officials treated health insurance funds as if these were spare resources that could be pulled out and redirected when convenient. 

That 60 billion pesos was meant for universal healthcare—expanding benefit packages, cutting premium burdens, and closing the gaps that force ordinary Filipinos to choose between buying medicine and buying food. Someone decided that money could be used for something else.

🟥 How the System Made It Possible

The way this fund transfer happened exposes how the system itself is built to enable this kind of maneuver while pretending everything is “by the book.”

It began with a special provision—Section 1(d) of the 2024 General Appropriations Act—inserted quietly at the bicameral conference committee stage. 

That clause did not appear in the original House or Senate versions of the budget. Yet in the final version, the Department of Finance suddenly had the power to sweep “excess funds” from government-owned and controlled corporations into the national treasury to finance unprogrammed appropriations.

In one sentence, inserted late in the process, Congress managed to override years of work on the Universal Health Care Act. 

That law was drafted, debated, and defended precisely to keep PhilHealth reserve funds from being raided. 

A budget provision, slipped in at the bicam level, tried to undo that protection without the same level of scrutiny.

As finance secretary, Recto issued a circular classifying around 89.9 billion pesos in PhilHealth reserves as “excess” and ordered their remittance to the treasury. Under Ledesma’s watch, PhilHealth complied. 

Between May and October 2024, 60 billion pesos moved out in three tranches; only a temporary restraining order from the Supreme Court stopped the remaining 29.9 billion from following.

Every step was wrapped in official clearances. The Office of the Government Corporate Counsel signed off, the Governance Commission for GOCCs agreed, the Commission on Audit did not stop it, and the PhilHealth board consented. Each office can now point to a document and say they were simply doing their part.

The Supreme Court did not buy the justification. 

In a unanimous decision, it called the move a grave abuse of discretion and declared that Congress cannot override the Universal Health Care Act or sin tax earmarking through a budget rider. The transfer and the special provision that authorized it were voided.

🟥 The “Good Faith” Shield

Recto’s defense is simple: Congress told him to do it, the President signed the budget, and as finance secretary he implemented what the law required.

Four Supreme Court justices went even further in that direction. In separate opinions, they emphasized that Recto acted in good faith, carried out a ministerial duty, and followed a law that, at that time, enjoyed the presumption of validity. Punishing him, they suggested, would be like punishing someone for performing the responsibilities of the office.

That line of reasoning creates a very convenient shield for top officials. 

As long as there is a law or budget line to point to, and as long as several agencies sign off, a cabinet secretary can participate in an unconstitutional operation and still be treated as blameless. The unconstitutional act becomes a purely abstract problem. The human beings who pushed it through remain untouched.

The problem is that the statutory prohibitions were clear. The Universal Health Care Act says PhilHealth reserve funds cannot be reverted to the national government’s general fund or used for purposes outside the health insurance program. 

Sin tax laws earmark several revenue sources specifically and exclusively for universal healthcare. Those rules were not hidden in some obscure regulation; they were central to the entire design of the PhilHealth fund.

The complaint against Recto and Ledesma argues that they knew about those prohibitions and went ahead anyway. 

Recto reclassified PhilHealth reserves as part of “excess GOCC funds” subject to transfer. Ledesma did not defend the integrity of the fund he was managing. 

Both, according to the complainants, helped turn a protected pool of health money into a pot that could be tapped for other priorities.

🟥 Follow the Money

The official explanation of where the 60 billion pesos went sounds reassuring at first glance, but falls apart once you look the details.

The Department of Finance says a large portion went to legitimate health-related uses: paying long-overdue Health Emergency Allowances for healthcare workers, funding medical assistance to poor patients, and buying equipment or improving facilities. On paper, those are defensible purposes.

But the same pool of money was also used to finance so-called “social determinants of health”—a vague label that, in practice, covered big-ticket infrastructure projects like major bridges, subway segments, and railway components. 

Those are important projects in their own right, but they are not PhilHealth expenditures in any meaningful sense. They are unprogrammed appropriations: a flexible reserve for favored projects.

This is where the anger from civil society groups is coming from. 

If the Supreme Court has already ruled that the fund transfer was unconstitutional, then the logical step is to return that same 60 billion pesos to PhilHealth. Instead, the plan is to restore the amount through future budget appropriations—meaning taxpayers will provide fresh money while no one is held to account for the original diversion.

Critics have described this as making the public pay twice. First, when health funds were taken away from their intended use. Second, when new tax money is allocated to replace what should never have been moved in the first place.

In short, it’s your money, they took it, they were ordered to return it, and instead of giving it back, you’re the one paying for their bullshit all over again.

🟥 What Was Lost in Health Care

While all of this played out at the policy and legal level, PhilHealth was not sitting on limitless surplus.

For 2024, PhilHealth requested over 100 billion pesos to support its National Health Insurance Program; the approved subsidy in the budget was much lower. On top of that, the government removed 60 billion pesos from the reserves. At the same time, the agency was expected to roll out the Universal Health Care law and widen its coverage and benefits.

Health experts have pointed out that full nationwide implementation of universal healthcare will require several hundred billion pesos more than what PhilHealth currently has. There is no true “excess” in that context; there is a gap between what the law promises and what the funding actually allows.

Senate and budget discussions over past years show the pattern clearly: what is requested for PhilHealth and what appears in the final law often do not match, with shortfalls of several billions each time. 

For 2025, PhilHealth’s subsidy was even removed at one stage in the bicameral process and then addressed later via realignments, including funds originally parked under the DPWH budget.

PhilHealth has been trying to show progress despite these constraints—expanding benefit packages, increasing coverage for conditions like pneumonia and cancer, and promoting zero-balance billing in more hospitals. Those gains are real. But they exist alongside the reality that its financial position was deliberately weakened by the decision to treat its reserves as a source of cash for other projects.

The key question is not whether PhilHealth managed to do something useful in 2024. The key question is what else could have been done—and how much more secure the health system could have been—if that 60 billion pesos had remained untouched, as the law intended.

🟥 Plunder, Technical Malversation, and the Law’s Blind Spots

The legal complaint against Recto and Ledesma leans on several laws: the Anti-Plunder Act, the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act, and the Revised Penal Code provision on technical malversation.

Plunder, by definition, requires that a public official amass ill-gotten wealth of at least 50 million pesos through a series of unlawful acts. That is a high bar. It demands not only proof of illegality in how funds were moved, but also evidence that the official personally accumulated wealth in the process.

That is where the case against Recto, in particular, will likely encounter resistance. The government can argue that the funds went to the national treasury and then to government programs, not into anyone’s private account. 

Four Supreme Court justices have already made it clear they do not see criminal liability on his part in the fund transfer, despite the transfer being voided.

Technical malversation has a different focus. It penalizes the use of public funds for purposes other than those for which they were appropriated. It does not require proof that the official became personally richer; it only requires proof that money earmarked for a specific purpose was knowingly applied to a different one.

On the face of it, taking reserve funds set aside for PhilHealth’s insurance mandate and using them for other government projects fits that description. That is why the complainants emphasize Section 11 of the Universal Health Care Act and the specific restrictions on PhilHealth’s reserves. They are trying to shift the center of gravity away from personal enrichment and toward misuse of protected funds.

The obstacle is the “good faith” doctrine. If senior officials can convince investigators, prosecutors, and judges that they simply followed a law they believed to be valid, and relied on the clearance of several oversight agencies, then the legal system may treat them as having committed an error of judgment, not a crime.

That reveals a blind spot in how the law is currently written. It is very good at chasing obvious thieves. It is much weaker when dealing with sophisticated, paper-heavy operations that move billions in public money under cover of formal compliance.

🟥 Accountability, or the Lack of It

Emmanuel Ledesma is already out of PhilHealth. His resignation was framed by the President as a personal difficulty with the political nature of the job, not as an admission of wrongdoing. There has been no clear acceptance of responsibility for authorizing the remittance of reserves.

Recto, far from being sidelined, has been elevated to Executive Secretary. Rather than being punished for his role in an unconstitutional transfer, he now occupies one of the most powerful positions in government, coordinating the entire cabinet.

Labor and civil society groups have called for his resignation. They accuse him of either not understanding social insurance funding or disregarding it. They have asked oversight institutions to investigate everyone who signed, cleared, or facilitated the transfer.

The structural problem is that responsibility is spread across numerous offices. Each one can say it relied on the others. Each can point to an approving memo, a legal opinion, or a budget line as cover. When everyone is involved, no single person is fully accountable.

That is exactly how systemic corruption operates in modern bureaucracies. It avoids the caricature of the lone thief stuffing envelopes. Instead, it uses procedure, paperwork, and institutional hierarchy to turn a series of formally correct steps into a massive substantive violation of the law’s purpose.

🟥 What This Says About Governance

The PhilHealth case is not only about 60 billion pesos. It reveals how fragile supposedly protected funds are when they stand in the way of political or fiscal expediency.

In theory, PhilHealth’s reserves exist to guarantee that the health insurance system can keep its promises, even during crises. In practice, those reserves were treated as a reservoir that could be tapped for other priorities, with “good faith” and inter-agency clearance as justification.

It took a unanimous Supreme Court decision to stop the bleeding and declare the operation unconstitutional. 

Even then, the remedy the court outlined—restoring the money through future budget processes—does little to resolve the question of who should be held accountable for authorizing the transfer in the first place.

The complaint for plunder and technical malversation may not succeed. The legal hurdles are high, and the system is already leaning toward treating this as a policy mistake rather than a criminal act.

But calling something “legal” after the fact does not make it right. Calling it “good faith” does not erase the fact that funds meant to secure people’s access to healthcare were treated as spare cash.

This is the real damage: ordinary Filipinos are told, once again, that the promises written into laws—universal healthcare, protected funds, social safety nets—are conditional. They hold only until someone in power finds a way, and a legal theory, to divert what was set aside for them.

If the law cannot fully recognize this kind of operation as plunder, then the flaw is not in the outrage people feel. The flaw is in a legal and political system that has not caught up with the more polished ways public money can be taken away from its true owners: the people it was supposed to serve.

178:
Being curious and pissed-off is really what started this for me: why does Leandro Leviste's name keep showing up in the DPWH scandal, and why does he seem to know so much?

As I went through reports and timelines, what I found was that he doesn't just appear as a brave whistleblower on the side. 

He has quietly positioned himself as the gatekeeper of the most explosive set of documents in this entire mess—and he is very clear that he will only move when a Cabinet secretary tells him to. 

The story he tells is neat. 

According to him, former DPWH undersecretary Maria Catalina Cabral gave him nationwide spreadsheets of DPWH "insertions" on September 4, 2024, after new Public Works Secretary Vince Dizon personally instructed her to hand them over "in the interest of transparency."

These files, by his own description, list the proponents of flood control and other DPWH projects across the whole government—congressmen, senators, Cabinet secretaries, undersecretaries, and even private individuals—over 17,000 line items for the 2025 budget alone. In other words, he doesn't just have a clue; he is sitting on the map.

At the same time, he keeps repeating one condition: he will only release these files to the public if Secretary Dizon allows it. 

He says he has already shown them to the Independent Commission for Infrastructure and to the Ombudsman, but for public disclosure, he defers upward to the same executive power that helped create and benefit from the system those files allegedly expose.

That setup benefits him politically far more than it benefits the public's right to know.

The more I read, the more that pattern bothered me. 

On one hand, Leviste talks about "wide‑ranging consequences" if the files are released, and frames himself as careful and responsible, worried about safety and due process.

On the other hand, the effect is very simple: he controls when, how, and how much of this evidence the public actually sees, while enjoying the political glow of being "the guy who has the list." 

In a system where almost everyone is implicated, the man holding the receipts suddenly has enormous soft power.

And even his cleanest claims have cracks. 

A DPWH security log from September 4 has been reported as contradicting his story that he walked out of Cabral's office carrying documents that day; the log notes his visit, and even records pizza being brought in, but reportedly doesn't show him leaving with papers.

Then you have that unverified but widely circulated Reddit allegation that he forced his way into Cabral's office and grabbed documents from her before a hearing—still unproven, but never squarely addressed or dispelled in a way that closes the question. 

For someone who talks so much about transparency, there are striking blind spots when it comes to his own actions.

And now there's Atty. Garrido's point, which adds another layer of concern. He argues that Leviste's unauthorized removal of Cabral's documents from her office may have rendered them inadmissible in court.

Official documents typically need authentication by the official custodian to be usable as evidence—through certified true copies or comparison with originals. 

Garrido says those files should never have left Cabral's office; at most they could have been duplicated and properly certified. 

With Cabral now dead, he asks: who certifies them as authentic? 

If they had stayed in the office, her successor could have authenticated them. 

In Leviste's hands, their legal status becomes questionable because he's neither DPWH nor part of the official investigation. 

Garrido's conclusion is that Leviste's meddling "for clout" may have made prosecution more difficult.

Meanwhile, Leviste's public framing is very careful. 

He constantly emphasizes that Dizon was the one who ordered the release of the files, and he repeats that he is only a custodian who followed instructions and later gave copies to the ICI and Ombudsman.

But the same statements also underline that, right now, he holds a version of those files and is choosing not to publish them. He says he can reveal "to the extent allowed in Congress," yet stops short of full disclosure. 

The way I see it, he's not powerless there; he's drawing that line on purpose.

And look at who is protected by that boundary. The files reportedly contain names of lawmakers, executive officials, and private actors across the country.

By delaying full publication, he effectively shields everyone on that list—good, bad, or somewhere in between—from immediate public scrutiny. 

That includes his colleagues, administration allies, and, by his own admission, HIS OWN MOTHER‼️

All of them stay in a kind of political limbo while he gets to speak as if he is above the game.

So after going through the coverage, the question in my head switches from "why does Leviste know so much?" to something sharper: "why is Leviste allowed to hold this much information hostage?"

The record shows he was given or obtained files that could blow open the entire DPWH insertion system. 

The record also shows that he has chosen to channel them upward to the same power structure that allegedly benefited from those insertions, and to drip‑feed only what is politically safe to disclose.

And this is where, personally, it stops being impressive and starts being infuriating. 

If you say you care about transparency but sit on documents that could finally make the system answerable, you are not helping the public—you are controlling the tempo of outrage. 

To me, that looks less like courage and more like holding crucial information hostage for political optics and personal positioning, and that gap between what he preaches and what he actually does is exactly what should make people more critical of his role, not less.

My take? Investigate this Promil kid. Pronto.

179:
Hi MCT. Curious lang ako – why does your writing look ChatGPT?
Part of what makes it look like an AI generated content are the red square emojis you are using.
Thanks.
– Jimmy

Hi Jimmy. Thanks for your email.

The red square emoji you’re talking about is actually a solution to a problem I had, back when I still had control over MCT 1.0.

If you’ve been following MCT for some time, you might remember that long post where I complained about Facebook’s lack of proper blog formatting. Facebook was never meant for long-form writing, and it shows.

The obvious option was to post everything on www.morningcoffeethoughts.org.

But here’s the catch.
Hostinger, my website host and web designer, doesn’t have an Android app. That means I can’t post on the fly. No quick edits. No spontaneous publishing.

So I looked for a workaround.

I asked ChatGPT what I could do since Facebook doesn’t support real headings. The suggestion was simple. Use a visual marker for headings. A square block.

Why red?

First, red is the MCT brand color.
Second, I have a color perception problem. I only clearly see a limited range of colors. Red, blue, and green. Red stands out the most. 

That’s the reason behind the red squares. Structure, readability, and accessibility. Nothing more.

Now, just to be clear.

Yes, I do use ChatGPT.
But not for writing.

I mostly use it to help format text so posts are easier to read.

I also have a love-hate relationship with ChatGPT. Part of that is personal. I lost 12 clients because of it.

Still, the tool is convenient, especially when I’m dealing with large amounts of data and trying to make sense of everything in my head.

But when it comes to writing, I have one rule.

My words.
My thoughts.
My ideas.

I don’t let ChatGPT write for me. Because like I said, my audience is myself. And if I let something else do the thinking and writing for me, para ko na ring niloko ang sarili ko.

For context, I’m a professional blogger. Between my own work and former clients, I’ve written a little over 5,000 blogs. Blogging paid the bills for years. It was my livelihood.

Then ChatGPT arrived, and I lost all my clients.

With my research and writing skills sitting idle, I decided to put them to use somewhere else.

That’s where Morning Coffee Thoughts comes in.

MCT was never meant to be a political page. I have always said I am not an analyst. I am not a pundit. Just a blogger expressing his thoughts while having coffee. Honestly, I was surprised people even started following it.

The reasons behind MCT are personal.

One, I was diagnosed with an early form of dementia. My doctor suggested I find an activity that could help sharpen my memory and slow its effects.

Second, I wanted to leave something behind for my daughter, just in case I’m no longer around.

Third, I’m 52 now and I don’t really have a personal legacy to leave behind.

And lastly, social media became too noisy. Fake news everywhere. Figuring out what’s true and what isn’t started to feel exhausting.

So MCT became my way of taking control of my learning.
For lack of a better word, it’s my thinking space.

I didn’t design MCT to convince anyone.
Its primary audience is me.

Para kapag dumating ang 2028, malinaw sa isip ko kung sino ang dapat kong iboto.

MCT isn’t here to impress anyone.
It’s here to help me remember who I am, what I believe, and why.

If it helps others, that’s a bonus.
If not, that’s okay too.
